        strategies.put("EMAIL", new EmailTask());
        strategies.put("DB", new DbWriteTask());
    }
    public static TaskStrategy get(String type) {
        return strategies.getOrDefault(type, p -> System.out.println("No-Op Task"));
    }
}

// Orchestrator Engine
public class WorkflowEngine {
    private final ExecutorService executor = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(4);

    public void processWorkflow(String taskType, String payload) {
        WorkflowContext ctx = new WorkflowContext();
        ctx.execute(); // Enter Running State

        executor.submit(() -> {
            TaskStrategy strategy = TaskFactory.get(taskType);
            strategy.executeTask(payload);
            
            ctx.setState(new CompletedState());
            ctx.execute(); // Transition to Completed State
        });
    }
    
    public void shutdown() { executor.shutdown(); }
}

Outline Singleton, Factory vs Abstract Factory, Strategy, Builder, Observer, Proxy, Decorator, and Adapter patterns.

These Gang of Four (GoF) patterns form the core of reusable object-oriented architectures:

  • Singleton: Restricts class instantiation to a single instance. Must prevent double-instantiation via multi-threading (using double-checked locking or JVM ClassLoader-based holder/enums).
  • Factory Method vs Abstract Factory: Factory Method uses inheritance to let subclasses decide which product to instantiate. Abstract Factory uses composition to create families of related products without specifying concrete classes.
  • Strategy: Defines a family of interchangeable algorithms and injects them dynamically. Promotes composition over inheritance.
  • Builder: Separates complex object construction from its representation, allowing step-by-step object assembly (essential for immutable structures).
  • Observer: Sets up a one-to-many subscription model so dependent objects receive automatic notifications when state changes.
  • Proxy vs Decorator vs Adapter:
    • **Proxy**: Controls access to the target object (e.g., lazy loading, security, caching, transactional aspects). Has the exact same interface.
    • **Decorator**: Adds responsibilities to an object dynamically without changing its interface.
    • **Adapter**: Converts an incompatible interface into another interface expected by client code.

Give a concrete example of a violation of the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) and how you refactored it using SOLID principles.

The Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) states that subtype objects must be substitutable for base type objects without altering correctness or breaking client expectations. A classic violation occurs when a subclass overrides a base class method but throws `UnsupportedOperationException`, narrows preconditions, or relaxes postconditions.

For example, if class `Bird` defines `fly()` and we write `Ostrich extends Bird`, overriding `fly()` to throw an exception, client code iterating over a list of `Bird` objects will break. We refactor this using:

  • Interface Segregation Principle (ISP): Split the interface into separate capabilities (`Flyable` and `NonFlyable` or just extract flying).
  • Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP): Program to abstractions, letting classes inject the capabilities they actually support.

SOLIDRefactoring.java 
// 🛑 LSP Violation Example:
// class Ostrich extends Bird { @Override public void fly() { throw new UnsupportedOperationException(); } }

// ✅ Refactored Solution using ISP and Composition
interface Flyable {
    void fly();
}

interface Walkable {
    void walk();
}

class Sparrow implements Flyable, Walkable {
    @Override public void fly() { System.out.println("Sparrow is flying."); }
    @Override public void walk() { System.out.println("Sparrow is walking."); }
}

class Ostrich implements Walkable {
    @Override public void walk() { System.out.println("Ostrich is walking fast."); }
}

// Client application interacts only with interfaces they require
public class SOLIDRefactoring {
    public static void makeFly(Flyable f) {
        f.fly(); // Safe, cannot receive an Ostrich here!
    }
}

What are the 4 pillars of OOP in Java?

Encapsulation: Restricts direct access to object states. Force interaction to occur through public methods (getters/setters), protecting internal data from corruption.

Abstraction: Hides complex implementation details and exposes only the essential features, reducing cognitive load.

Inheritance: Promotes code reuse by allowing subclasses to inherit fields and behaviors from parent classes.

Polymorphism: The ability for different classes to respond to the same method call in their own way (method overloading and overriding).

OopPillarsDemo.java 
// Abstraction (Abstract Class) and Encapsulation (Private fields)
abstract class Animal {
    private String name; // Encapsulated field
    public Animal(String name) { this.name = name; }
    public String getName() { return name; }
    public abstract void makeSound(); // Abstraction
}

// Inheritance (Dog extends Animal) and Polymorphism (sound override)
class Dog extends Animal {
    public Dog(String name) { super(name); }
    @Override
    public void makeSound() { System.out.println("Woof"); } // Polymorphism
}

What is the Liskov Substitution Principle?

Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP): Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types without altering the correctness or behavior of the application.

DIP (Dependency Inversion): Depend on abstractions (interfaces) rather than concrete implementations, keeping components loosely coupled.

Interface Segregation (ISP): Prefer small, client-specific interfaces over massive, general-purpose interfaces.

LspCompliant.java 
// LSP Violation: class Ostrich extends Bird { void fly() { throw new Unsupported(); } }
// LSP Compliant: split interfaces based on behavior capabilities:
interface Walkable { void walk(); }
interface Flyable { void fly(); }

class Ostrich implements Walkable {
    @Override public void walk() { System.out.println("Ostrich walking..."); }
}

What is the Singleton pattern? How do you implement it in Java?

Singleton Pattern: Ensures a class has only one instance per JVM classloader and provides a global access point to it.

Double-Checked Locking: Uses a synchronized block inside a null check to initialize the instance lazily. The instance variable must be marked volatile to prevent instruction reordering bugs.

Bill Pugh Holder: The preferred lazy-initialization approach. It leverages a private static inner helper class, which is loaded only when getInstance() is invoked, guaranteeing thread-safety without synchronization overhead.

BillPughSingleton.java 
public class BillPughSingleton {
    private BillPughSingleton() {} // Prevent direct instantiation

    private static class SingletonHelper {
        private static final BillPughSingleton INSTANCE = new BillPughSingleton();
    }

    public static BillPughSingleton getInstance() {
        return SingletonHelper.INSTANCE; // Lazy loaded and thread-safe
    }
}

What is the Strategy pattern?

Strategy Pattern: Defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them interchangeable. It allows the algorithm to vary independently from clients that use it.

Loose Coupling: Promotes the Open/Closed Principle: you can add new strategy classes without modifying the host context class.

Spring Integration: Easily implemented in Spring by autowiring a List or Map of strategy beans, and selecting the active strategy dynamically at runtime.

PaymentStrategyDemo.java 
import java.util.*;

interface PaymentStrategy { void pay(double amount); }
class CardPayment implements PaymentStrategy { public void pay(double a) { System.out.println("Paid: " + a); } }

class OrderProcessor {
    private PaymentStrategy strategy;
    public void setPaymentStrategy(PaymentStrategy s) { this.strategy = s; }
    
    public void process(double total) {
        strategy.pay(total); // Interchangeable algorithm execution
    }
}

Where is the Builder pattern commonly used?

Overview: The Builder pattern is commonly used when constructing complex objects with many optional parameters (e.g., configuring HTTP clients, creating domain entities, or using Lombok's @Builder). It avoids having constructor overload explosions and enforces object immutability by setting all attributes before calling build().

Enterprise Standard: In production environments, this behavior must be carefully tested, integrated into CI/CD pipelines, and monitored via standard observability tools.

Best Practice: Keep configurations modular, optimize resource utilization, handle exceptions gracefully, and implement proper logging/metrics trackers.

EnterpriseSnippet.java 
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

public class EnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(EnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void executeLogic() {
        log.info("Processing business flow using standard patterns...");
    }
}

What is the Observer pattern?

Overview: The Observer pattern defines a one-to-many dependency where an object (the subject) maintains a list of dependents (observers) and notifies them automatically of any state changes. Common use cases include event listeners, model-view updating, and reactive programming.

Enterprise Standard: In production environments, this behavior must be carefully tested, integrated into CI/CD pipelines, and monitored via standard observability tools.

Best Practice: Keep configurations modular, optimize resource utilization, handle exceptions gracefully, and implement proper logging/metrics trackers.

EnterpriseSnippet.java 
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

public class EnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(EnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void executeLogic() {
        log.info("Processing business flow using standard patterns...");
    }
}

Explain the Proxy pattern with real-world usage.

Proxy Pattern: Provides a surrogate or placeholder object to control access to the target object. It intercepts method calls to inject auxiliary behaviors (like logging, security check, transaction management, or lazy loading).

Spring AOP Proxies: Spring creates JDK dynamic proxies (for interface-based beans) or CGLIB proxies (for class-based beans) to wrap target beans and handle `@Transactional` or `@Cacheable` advice.

Self-Invocation Gotcha: If a method inside a bean calls another method within the same bean, the call bypasses the AOP proxy wrapper, meaning aspects like `@Transactional` will not execute.

SpringAopProxyDemo.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;
import org.springframework.transaction.annotation.Transactional;

@Service
public class SpringAopProxyDemo {
    
    public void outerMethod() {
        innerMethod(); // Gotcha: self-invocation bypasses transaction interceptor
    }

    @Transactional
    public void innerMethod() {
        // SQL updates...
    }
}

What is the Template Method pattern?

Template Method: Defines the skeleton of an algorithm in an abstract superclass method, deferring specific implementation steps to subclass overrides.

Algorithm Skeleton: The skeleton method is typically marked `final` to prevent subclasses from modifying the execution flow.

Hook Methods: Subclasses override abstract or optional hook methods to plug custom logic into the template flow.

DataExporter.java 
public abstract class DataExporter {
    // Template Method defines algorithm skeleton
    public final void exportData() {
        readSource();
        String formatted = formatData();
        writeFile(formatted);
    }

    protected abstract void readSource();
    protected abstract String formatData();
    private void writeFile(String data) { System.out.println("Writing to disk..."); }
}

When do you use Command pattern?

Command Pattern: Encapsulates a request as an object, decoupling the invoker from the receiver. It supports queuing requests, transaction logs, and undo/redo systems.

Encapsulated Action: The Command object exposes a single method (like `execute()`) and maintains references to the target receiver and input parameters.

CommandPatternDemo.java 
interface Command { void execute(); }
class Light { public void turnOn() { System.out.println("Light ON"); } }

class LightOnCommand implements Command {
    private final Light light;
    public LightOnCommand(Light l) { this.light = l; }
    @Override public void execute() { light.turnOn(); }
}

What’s the benefit of Chain of Responsibility?

Chain of Responsibility: Avoids coupling the sender of a request to its receiver by giving more than one object a chance to handle the request.

Handler Chain: Handlers are linked in a chain. Each handler decides whether to process the request or pass it to the next link.

Enterprise Usage: Standard examples include Servlet Filter chains, Spring Security filters, and logging frameworks.

FilterChainDemo.java 
abstract class Handler {
    protected Handler next;
    public void setNext(Handler h) { this.next = h; }
    public abstract void handleRequest(String request);
}

class AuthHandler extends Handler {
    @Override
    public void handleRequest(String r) {
        if (r.contains("VALID_AUTH")) {
            if (next != null) next.handleRequest(r);
        }
    }
}

Example of Flyweight pattern in Java APIs?

Flyweight Pattern: Minimizes memory usage by sharing state elements across multiple objects instead of recreating matching objects.

Intrinsic State: Shared constant state (stored inside the flyweight object).

Java API caching: Common flyweight examples include the String constant pool, and autoboxing caches (like `Integer.valueOf()` caching values -128 to 127).

IntegerCacheDemo.java 
public class IntegerCacheDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Reuses cached Integer instances (Flyweight)
        Integer a = Integer.valueOf(100);
        Integer b = Integer.valueOf(100);
        System.out.println("Same instance: " + (a == b)); // true

        // Bypasses cache, creates new objects
        Integer c = Integer.valueOf(200);
        Integer d = Integer.valueOf(200);
        System.out.println("Same instance: " + (c == d)); // false
    }
}

What pattern does Java’s Enum Singleton use?

Singleton Pattern: Ensures a class has only one instance per JVM classloader and provides a global access point to it.

Double-Checked Locking: Uses a synchronized block inside a null check to initialize the instance lazily. The instance variable must be marked volatile to prevent instruction reordering bugs.

Bill Pugh Holder: The preferred lazy-initialization approach. It leverages a private static inner helper class, which is loaded only when getInstance() is invoked, guaranteeing thread-safety without synchronization overhead.

BillPughSingleton.java 
public class BillPughSingleton {
    private BillPughSingleton() {} // Prevent direct instantiation

    private static class SingletonHelper {
        private static final BillPughSingleton INSTANCE = new BillPughSingleton();
    }

    public static BillPughSingleton getInstance() {
        return SingletonHelper.INSTANCE; // Lazy loaded and thread-safe
    }
}

How to test code that uses Factory or Strategy pattern?

Factory Method: Defines an interface or abstract class for creating objects, but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate, encapsulating object creation logic.

Abstract Factory: Provides an interface for creating families of related or dependent objects without specifying their concrete classes.

Selection Criteria: Use Factory Method for creating single objects of a polymorphic type. Switch to Abstract Factory when you need to coordinate multiple related polymorphic objects (like UI button and panel styling families).

FactoryDemo.java 
interface Shape { void draw(); }
class Circle implements Shape { @Override public void draw() { System.out.println("Circle"); } }
class Square implements Shape { @Override public void draw() { System.out.println("Square"); } }

class ShapeFactory {
    public static Shape getShape(String type) {
        if ("CIRCLE".equalsIgnoreCase(type)) return new Circle();
        if ("SQUARE".equalsIgnoreCase(type)) return new Square();
        throw new IllegalArgumentException("Unknown shape: " + type);
    }
}

Name Some of the Design Patterns Used in the Spring Framework?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}
Inversion of Control

Spring Core & AOP

ApplicationContext vs BeanFactory, bean lifecycle and scopes (gotchas with prototype in singleton), Dependency Injection patterns, Aspect-Oriented Programming (Aspect, Advice, Weaving), and proxy self-invocation gotchas.

Why is Spring AOP used in enterprise applications? Show how to create a custom aspect to log method executions.

Aspect-Oriented Programming: Spring AOP decouples cross-cutting concerns (logging, transaction management, security checks) from core business logic, preventing duplicate code.

Proxy Architecture: AOP wraps target beans in dynamic proxies at runtime (using JDK dynamic proxies or CGLIB). Calls are intercepted to run advice before delegating to the target method.

Self-Invocation gotcha: When a bean invokes its own method internally, the call bypasses the proxy container, meaning aspects (like @Transactional or custom logging aspects) will not execute.

Aspect Mechanics: Aspects are defined using pointcut expressions that identify execution targets, and advice (like @Before or @Around) that determines when the aspect logic runs.

ExecutionTimeAspect.java 
import org.aspectj.lang.ProceedingJoinPoint;
import org.aspectj.lang.annotation.Around;
import org.aspectj.lang.annotation.Aspect;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Aspect
@Component
public class ExecutionTimeAspect {

    // Aspect intercepts all methods inside services package
    @Around("execution(* com.valore.grocery.service..*(..))")
    public Object profileMethod(ProceedingJoinPoint joinPoint) throws Throwable {
        long start = System.currentTimeMillis();
        
        try {
            // Execute actual service method
            return joinPoint.proceed();
        } finally {
            long executionTime = System.currentTimeMillis() - start;
            System.out.println(joinPoint.getSignature() + " executed in " + executionTime + "ms");
        }
    }
}

Explain the roles of @Qualifier and @Override annotations. Compare @NoArgsConstructor and @AllArgsConstructor in Lombok.

@Qualifier: Resolves injection ambiguity when multiple beans of the same type exist in the context. By pairing it with @Autowired, you specify the exact bean name to inject.

@Override: A compiler-checked annotation ensuring a subclass method signature matches a method in the superclass or interface, preventing accidental signature modifications.

@NoArgsConstructor: Generates a zero-argument constructor, which is necessary for JPA entities (Hibernate proxy generation) and JSON serialization.

@AllArgsConstructor: Generates a constructor containing parameters for all fields, enabling clean constructor-based Dependency Injection in Spring components.

ConstructorInjectionQualifierDemo.java 
import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Qualifier;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import lombok.AllArgsConstructor;
import lombok.NoArgsConstructor;

interface NotificationSender { void send(String msg); }

@Component("emailSender")
class EmailSender implements NotificationSender {
    @Override // Forces compilation validation for overrides
    public void send(String msg) { System.out.println("Email: " + msg); }
}

@Component("smsSender")
class SmsSender implements NotificationSender {
    @Override
    public void send(String msg) { System.out.println("SMS: " + msg); }
}

@Component
@AllArgsConstructor // Generates constructor for injection (no @Autowired needed on constructor)
class AlertService {
    
    // Disambiguate using @Qualifier
    @Qualifier("emailSender")
    private final NotificationSender emailNotifier;
    
    @Qualifier("smsSender")
    private final NotificationSender smsNotifier;

    public void triggerAlert(String text) {
        emailNotifier.send(text);
        smsNotifier.send(text);
    }
}

What is the difference between Spring's Singleton and Prototype scopes? How do you resolve the prototype-in-singleton injection gotcha?

Singleton Scope: Spring's default scope. The container creates exactly one bean instance per IoC context. Subsequent requests return the cached instance, which requires stateless implementations to ensure thread safety.

Prototype Scope: Instructs the container to create a new instance of the bean every time it is requested, making it suitable for stateful business workflows.

Scoped Bean Gotcha: When a prototype bean is injected into a singleton bean, it is only injected once during the singleton's initialization. The singleton bean continues to reuse that initial instance, preventing updates.

Resolution Strategies: Resolve this by accessing the prototype bean programmatically using ObjectProvider<T>, or by annotating a getter method with @Lookup to fetch a new instance from the container at runtime.

PrototypeInjectionSolution.java 
import org.springframework.beans.factory.ObjectProvider;
import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Autowired;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Scope;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Component
@Scope("prototype")
class TaskProcessor {
    private double taskId = Math.random();
    public void process() {
        System.out.println("Processing Task ID: " + taskId);
    }
}

@Component
class SingletonManager {
    // Injected provider - allows fetching fresh prototype instances on demand
    @Autowired
    private ObjectProvider<TaskProcessor> processorProvider;

    public void runTask() {
        // Fetch a brand new TaskProcessor instance every time this method runs
        TaskProcessor processor = processorProvider.getObject();
        processor.process();
    }
}

Explain Inversion of Control (IoC) vs Dependency Injection (DI). Compare BeanFactory vs ApplicationContext.

Inversion of Control (IoC): IoC is an architectural design principle where control over object creation, configuration, and lifecycle management is transferred from the application code to an external container or framework.

Dependency Injection (DI): DI is the implementation pattern used to realize IoC. Instead of objects instantiating their dependencies directly, dependencies are injected into them by the Spring container via constructor, setter, or field injection.

BeanFactory: The foundational IoC interface in Spring. It lazy-loads beans by default, features a small memory footprint, and provides basic configuration support. It is suitable for resource-constrained environments.

ApplicationContext: Extends BeanFactory, loading all singleton beans eagerly at startup. It adds advanced enterprise features like internationalization (i18n), event publishing, WebApplicationContext bindings, and seamless integration with AOP.

IocDiDemoConfig.java 
import org.springframework.context.ApplicationContext;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.AnnotationConfigApplicationContext;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration;

@Configuration
class AppConfig {
    @Bean
    public PaymentService paymentService() {
        return new CreditCardPaymentService(); // Dependency Instantiation delegated to Spring
    }
}

interface PaymentService { void pay(); }
class CreditCardPaymentService implements PaymentService {
    @Override public void pay() { System.out.println("Processing credit card charge..."); }
}

public class IocDiDemoConfig {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Instantiate Spring ApplicationContext container
        ApplicationContext context = new AnnotationConfigApplicationContext(AppConfig.class);
        
        // Retrieve bean from IoC container (DI resolves dependency)
        PaymentService service = context.getBean(PaymentService.class);
        service.pay();
    }
}

Explain the self-invocation proxy gotcha in Spring AOP and how to bypass it. Show a custom Aspect for distributed rate limiting.

Spring AOP is proxy-based. When a client bean calls a method on another target bean, Spring routes the call through an intermediate proxy, executing any woven aspects (like `@Transactional` or custom rate limiters). However, if a bean calls one of its own internal methods (self-invocation), the call bypasses the proxy entirely, resolving to direct JVM `this` reference calls. As a result, **no aspect logic executes**.

Bypassing Self-Invocation:
  1. Self-Injection (Recommended): Inject the bean into itself using lazy autowiring. Internal calls are made on the self-injected variable, routing through the proxy.
  2. AopContext.currentProxy(): Access the proxy directly via `((MyService) AopContext.currentProxy()).internalMethod()`. Requires setting `@EnableAspectJAutoProxy(exposeProxy = true)`.
  3. Refactoring: Move the self-invoked method to a separate helper helper service bean.
RateLimiterAspect.java 
import org.aspectj.lang.ProceedingJoinPoint;
import org.aspectj.lang.annotation.Around;
import org.aspectj.lang.annotation.Aspect;
import org.aspectj.lang.annotation.Pointcut;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Lazy;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import java.util.concurrent.ConcurrentHashMap;

@Aspect
@Component
public class RateLimiterAspect {
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, Long> clientRequestTimestamps = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
    private final MyService self;

    // Self-injection to bypass self-invocation gotcha if needed!
    public RateLimiterAspect(@Lazy MyService self) {
        this.self = self;
    }

    @Pointcut("@annotation(RateLimit)")
    public void rateLimitedMethod() {}

    @Around("rateLimitedMethod()")
    public Object enforceLimit(ProceedingJoinPoint joinPoint) throws Throwable {
        // Mocking client tracking (In production, retrieve IP/JWT token from SecurityContext)
        String clientIp = "127.0.0.1"; 
        
        long currentTime = System.currentTimeMillis();
        long lastRequest = clientRequestTimestamps.getOrDefault(clientIp, 0L);

        if (currentTime - lastRequest < 1000) { // Limit: 1 request per second
            throw new RuntimeException("HTTP 429: Too Many Requests. Rate limit exceeded.");
        }

        clientRequestTimestamps.put(clientIp, currentTime);
        return joinPoint.proceed(); // Safe execution
    }
}

What is Inversion of Control (IoC) and Dependency Injection (DI)? Compare BeanFactory vs ApplicationContext and Bean Scopes.

IoC (Inversion of Control) is the design principal where control over object creation, configuration, and lifecycles is inverted and handed over to a container framework. Dependency Injection (DI) is the concrete implementation pattern used to supply dependencies to objects. Spring supports Field, Setter, and Constructor injection. **Constructor injection is the industry best practice** because it ensures dependencies are immutable, facilitates unit testing without mocking containers, and prevents circular dependencies at startup.

BeanFactory vs ApplicationContext
  • BeanFactory: The root Spring container interface. Employs **Lazy Loading** (instantiates beans only when `getBean()` is explicitly requested). Extremely lightweight, suitable for memory-constrained environments.
  • ApplicationContext: Inherits from BeanFactory. Employs **Eager Loading** (pre-instantiates all singleton beans during startup). Extends functionality with advanced enterprise integrations: event propagation, internalization (i18n), AOP integration, and environment profile configurations.
Bean Scopes:
  • singleton: (Default) Exactly one instance of the bean is created per Spring IoC container.
  • prototype: A new bean instance is created every single time it is requested from the container.
  • request / session / application: Web-aware scopes bound to the lifecycle of HTTP requests, HTTP sessions, and ServletContext respectively.
Gotcha: Prototype Inside Singleton

If a prototype-scoped bean is injected directly (e.g. via @Autowired) into a singleton-scoped bean, **it behaves like a singleton**. The singleton bean is initialized only once, meaning the prototype bean is also injected only once. To resolve this, use @Lookup method injection or inject a Java Provider<T> / ObjectFactory<T> to fetch fresh instances dynamically.

PrototypeLookupPattern.java 
import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Lookup;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Scope;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Component
@Scope("prototype")
class RequestTracker {
    public void logRequest(String msg) {
        System.out.println("Tracker: " + this.hashCode() + " | " + msg);
    }
}

@Component
public class RequestService {
    // Spring dynamically overrides this method to retrieve a new prototype bean instance
    @Lookup
    public RequestTracker getTracker() {
        return null; // Implemented by Spring proxy
    }

    public void process(String requestData) {
        RequestTracker tracker = getTracker(); // Fetches a fresh instance every time!
        tracker.logRequest(requestData);
    }
}

Difference between synchronized and ReentrantLock?

synchronized keyword: An intrinsic locking mechanism built into the JVM. It is easy to use, handles lock acquisition and release implicitly, and operates on the object monitor level. However, it lacks advanced configuration parameters and cannot be interrupted during acquisition.

ReentrantLock class: Part of the java.util.concurrent.locks package. It provides rich extensions such as fair locking, interruptible lock waiting, and tryLock with a timeout. It requires explicit unlocking in a finally block to prevent lock leakage.

Performance comparison: While intrinsic lock performance is optimized in modern JVMs, ReentrantLock remains the standard choice for complex, high-concurrency enterprise pipelines requiring timed locks.

LockComparisonDemo.java 
import java.util.concurrent.locks.ReentrantLock;
import java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit;

public class LockComparisonDemo {
    private final ReentrantLock lock = new ReentrantLock(true); // Fair lock

    public void safeExecute() {
        try {
            // Attempt to acquire lock within 500ms timeout
            if (lock.tryLock(500, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS)) {
                try {
                    System.out.println("Executing business logic safely...");
                } finally {
                    lock.unlock(); // Explicit release is mandatory
                }
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
        }
    }
}

What’s the difference between ArrayList and LinkedList?

ArrayList: Backed by a contiguous dynamic array. It offers fast O(1) random access index lookups. However, inserting or deleting elements from arbitrary positions requires O(N) shifts in memory.

LinkedList: Structured as a doubly-linked list. It provides O(1) insertions or deletions at endpoints, but O(N) random access search because it must traverse nodes from head or tail.

Cache Locality: ArrayList is far faster in practice due to CPU cache locality (elements reside in contiguous memory slots), whereas LinkedList nodes are scattered, causing CPU cache misses.

ListPerformanceComparison.java 
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.LinkedList;
import java.util.List;

public class ListPerformanceComparison {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List<Integer> arrayList = new ArrayList<>();
        List<Integer> linkedList = new LinkedList<>();

        // ArrayList is highly optimized for index-based retrieval
        arrayList.add(10); 
        int val = arrayList.get(0); // O(1)

        // LinkedList is optimized for queue/deque behaviors
        linkedList.add(0, 20); // O(1) insertion at head
    }
}

How do TreeMap and HashMap differ?

Node Array Buckets: A HashMap is backed by an array of Node<K,V> buckets. Keys are mapped to buckets using a hash function: first computing the key's hash code, spreading it to reduce collision rates, and resolving the target index with a bitwise AND.

Collision Chaining: When multiple keys resolve to the same index, they are stored in a singly linked list inside that bucket (O(N) search complexity).

Treeification: In Java 8+, if a bucket's size exceeds 8 and the table capacity is at least 64, the linked list converts into a balanced Red-Black Tree, improving search complexity to O(log N).

HashMapInternalsDemo.java 
import java.util.HashMap;

public class HashMapInternalsDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        HashMap<String, Integer> map = new HashMap<>(16, 0.75f);
        
        // 1. Key hash is computed: (h = key.hashCode()) ^ (h >>> 16)
        // 2. Bucket index resolved: index = hash & (n - 1)
        map.put("Valore", 100);
        
        System.out.println("Value: " + map.get("Valore"));
    }
}

Difference between composition and inheritance?

Composition ("has-a"): A strong relationship where the contained object's lifecycle is owned by the container (e.g. Engine inside Car; if Car is destroyed, Engine is destroyed).

Aggregation ("has-a"): A weak relationship where the contained object exists independently of the container (e.g. Department has Employees; if Department is destroyed, Employees remain).

Inheritance ("is-a"): Extends classes, but creates tight coupling. Composition is preferred over inheritance because it keeps components loosely coupled and allows changing behaviors dynamically at runtime.

CompositionDemo.java 
class Engine { void start() {} }

class Car {
    private final Engine engine; // Composition: Car owns Engine lifecycle

    public Car() {
        this.engine = new Engine();
    }
    
    public void drive() { engine.start(); }
}

What is the diamond problem? How does Java 8 handle it?

Diamond Problem: Occurs in multiple inheritance architectures when a subclass inherits conflicting method implementations from two separate parent routes.

Java Interface Solution: Since Java 8 supports default methods in interfaces, a class implementing two interfaces defining matching method signatures will encounter this conflict.

Explicit Override: To resolve this conflict, the implementing class must explicitly override the colliding method and specify which interface's method to invoke using the `super` keyword.

DiamondResolution.java 
interface InterfaceA {
    default void execute() { System.out.println("InterfaceA executing"); }
}

interface InterfaceB {
    default void execute() { System.out.println("InterfaceB executing"); }
}

public class DiamondResolution implements InterfaceA, InterfaceB {
    // Resolve conflict by overriding and specifying target route
    @Override
    public void execute() {
        InterfaceA.super.execute(); // Or InterfaceB.super.execute()
    }
}

How does G1GC differ from CMS?

G1GC (Garbage-First): The default collector since Java 9. It divides the heap into thousands of equal-sized regions. It prioritizes regions containing the most garbage, compacting them concurrently during configurable pause times.

CMS (Concurrent Mark Sweep): A legacy collector focused on low pause times. It did not compact the heap, causing fragmentation that eventually triggered stop-the-world Full GCs. CMS was deprecated and completely removed in Java 14.

ZGC: A scalable, ultra-low latency collector. It performs all garbage collection phases concurrently with application threads, keeping pauses under a millisecond even on multi-terabyte heaps.

GcTuningConfigs.sh 
#!/bin/bash
# Enable G1GC with a 200ms target pause limit
java -XX:+UseG1GC -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200 -jar app.jar

Difference between map() and flatMap() in Streams?

Overview: map() transforms each element. flatMap() flattens nested structures.

Enterprise Standard: In production environments, this behavior must be carefully tested, integrated into CI/CD pipelines, and monitored via standard observability tools.

Best Practice: Keep configurations modular, optimize resource utilization, handle exceptions gracefully, and implement proper logging/metrics trackers.

EnterpriseSnippet.java 
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

public class EnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(EnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void executeLogic() {
        log.info("Processing business flow using standard patterns...");
    }
}

Difference between @Component, @Service, @Repository?

Stereotype Annotations: Annotations that mark classes as Spring-managed beans, registering them in the application context during component scans.

@Component: The generic root stereotype annotation indicating a class is a Spring bean.

@Service: Used on service classes, indicating business transaction components.

@Repository: Used on data access components, indicating integration with database storage and automatically translating raw SQL exceptions into Spring's unified `DataAccessException` hierarchy.

StereotypeBean.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Repository;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;

@Repository
class UserRepository {
    // Data access logic...
}

@Service
public class StereotypeBean {
    private final UserRepository repo;
    public StereotypeBean(UserRepository r) { this.repo = r; }
}

How does Spring manage bean lifecycle?

Cycle Detection: In directed graphs, a cycle exists if there is a back-edge during traversal. This is resolved using a Depth-First Search (DFS) tracking vertex visiting states.

Three-State Coloring: Mark nodes with states: (1) Unvisited: not yet processed; (2) Visiting: currently in the active DFS recursion stack; (3) Visited: fully processed.

Cycle Identification: If during DFS traversal of a node's neighbors we encounter a neighbor marked as **Visiting**, a back-edge is present, indicating a cycle.

CycleDetector.java 
import java.util.*;

public class CycleDetector {
    public boolean hasCycle(int v, List<List<Integer>> adj) {
        int[] visited = new int[v]; // 0: unvisited, 1: visiting, 2: visited
        for (int i = 0; i < v; i++) {
            if (visited[i] == 0) {
                if (dfs(i, adj, visited)) return true;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }

    private boolean dfs(int node, List<List<Integer>> adj, int[] visited) {
        visited[node] = 1; // Mark as visiting
        for (int neighbor : adj.get(node)) {
            if (visited[neighbor] == 1) return true; // Cycle detected
            if (visited[neighbor] == 0) {
                if (dfs(neighbor, adj, visited)) return true;
            }
        }
        visited[node] = 2; // Mark as fully visited
        return false;
    }
}

What is the role of @ConditionalOnProperty?

Conditional Bean Registration: Spring provides the `@Conditional` annotation to register beans dynamically in the application context based on specific runtime conditions.

Common Variations: Spring Boot defines multiple derived annotations like `@ConditionalOnProperty` (checks configuration properties), `@ConditionalOnClass` (checks classpath files), and `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` (registers a bean only if no other bean of the same type exists).

ConditionalConfig.java 
import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.condition.ConditionalOnProperty;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration;

@Configuration
public class ConditionalConfig {
    @Bean
    // Only registered if 'feature.audit.enabled' is set to true in properties
    @ConditionalOnProperty(name = "feature.audit.enabled", havingValue = "true")
    public AuditService auditService() {
        return new AuditService();
    }
}
class AuditService {}

What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous calls?

Synchronous: The calling thread blocks, waiting for a response from the downstream service (e.g. RestTemplate HTTP calls). This wastes resources and can cause thread pool starvation under load.

Asynchronous: The calling thread returns immediately, executing operations non-blockingly via callbacks or future events (e.g. using `CompletableFuture` or reactive Streams).

AsyncExecutor.java 
import org.springframework.scheduling.annotation.Async;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;
import java.util.concurrent.CompletableFuture;

@Service
public class AsyncExecutor {
    @Async // Executes asynchronously on a separate thread pool
    public CompletableFuture<String> executeTask() {
        // Long running task...
        return CompletableFuture.completedFuture("Task Result");
    }
}

How is Factory pattern different from Abstract Factory?

Factory Method: Defines an interface or abstract class for creating objects, but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate, encapsulating object creation logic.

Abstract Factory: Provides an interface for creating families of related or dependent objects without specifying their concrete classes.

Selection Criteria: Use Factory Method for creating single objects of a polymorphic type. Switch to Abstract Factory when you need to coordinate multiple related polymorphic objects (like UI button and panel styling families).

FactoryDemo.java 
interface Shape { void draw(); }
class Circle implements Shape { @Override public void draw() { System.out.println("Circle"); } }
class Square implements Shape { @Override public void draw() { System.out.println("Square"); } }

class ShapeFactory {
    public static Shape getShape(String type) {
        if ("CIRCLE".equalsIgnoreCase(type)) return new Circle();
        if ("SQUARE".equalsIgnoreCase(type)) return new Square();
        throw new IllegalArgumentException("Unknown shape: " + type);
    }
}

Difference between Adapter and Decorator pattern?

Adapter Pattern: Converts the interface of a class into another interface clients expect, enabling classes with incompatible interfaces to collaborate.

Decorator Pattern: Wraps an object to add new responsibilities or behaviors dynamically without altering the original interface.

Key Difference: Adapter changes an object's interface to resolve compatibility issues, while Decorator extends an object's behavior while keeping the interface identical.

DecoratorDemo.java 
interface Message { String getContent(); }
class SimpleMessage implements Message { public String getContent() { return "Hello"; } }

// Decorator wraps target message and extends behavior
class EncryptedMessageDecorator implements Message {
    private final Message target;
    public EncryptedMessageDecorator(Message m) { this.target = m; }
    
    @Override
    public String getContent() {
        return Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString(target.getContent().getBytes());
    }
}

What is Dependency Injection? How does Spring use it?

Inversion of Control (IoC): Delegates control of bean configuration, instantiation, and dependency wiring to an external container (Spring IoC Container).

Dependency Injection (DI): The pattern used to implement IoC. Instead of objects instantiating dependencies directly, Spring supplies dependencies via constructor, setter, or field injection.

Best Practice: Constructor injection is preferred because it enforces immutability, facilitates unit testing using mock objects, and prevents partial bean construction bugs.

ConstructorInjection.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

interface Engine { void run(); }

@Component
public class ConstructorInjection {
    private final Engine engine; // Immutable final field

    // Constructor injection (Autowired is optional since Spring 4.3)
    public ConstructorInjection(Engine engine) {
        this.engine = engine;
    }
}

How is MVC implemented in Spring?

DispatcherServlet: The central Front Controller. It intercepts all incoming HTTP requests and delegates processing to HandlerMappings.

HandlerMapping & Controller: HandlerMapping identifies the correct `@Controller` method matching the request path. The controller executes business logic, returning data or a view name.

ViewResolver: Resolves view names (like JSP or Thymeleaf templates) and renders the response. For REST endpoints, `@RestController` writes JSON directly to the HTTP response body using HttpMessageConverters.

RestMvcController.java 
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.GetMapping;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RestController;

@RestController // Combines @Controller and @ResponseBody
public class RestMvcController {
    
    @GetMapping("/api/status")
    public Status getStatus() {
        return new Status("ACTIVE"); // Auto-serialized to JSON via Jackson
    }
}
class Status {
    private String val;
    public Status(String v) { this.val = v; }
    public String getVal() { return val; }
}

What is AOP? Common use cases?

Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP): Decouples cross-cutting concerns (logging, transaction management, caching, security checks) from core business logic, preventing code duplication.

Aspect & Advice: An aspect defines a modularized concern. Advice determines the action taken (e.g. `@Before`, `@After`, or `@Around` method execution).

Pointcut: A regular expression that identifies execution targets (method patterns) where advice should apply.

LoggingAspect.java 
import org.aspectj.lang.ProceedingJoinPoint;
import org.aspectj.lang.annotation.Around;
import org.aspectj.lang.annotation.Aspect;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Aspect
@Component
public class LoggingAspect {
    // Intercepts execution of all service methods
    @Around("execution(* com.myapp.service.*.*(..))")
    public Object logExecutionTime(ProceedingJoinPoint joinPoint) throws Throwable {
        long start = System.currentTimeMillis();
        Object proceed = joinPoint.proceed(); // Delegate to target method
        long executionTime = System.currentTimeMillis() - start;
        System.out.println(joinPoint.getSignature() + " ran in " + executionTime + "ms");
        return proceed;
    }
}

What’s the difference between Composition and Aggregation?

Composition ("has-a"): A strong relationship where the contained object's lifecycle is owned by the container (e.g. Engine inside Car; if Car is destroyed, Engine is destroyed).

Aggregation ("has-a"): A weak relationship where the contained object exists independently of the container (e.g. Department has Employees; if Department is destroyed, Employees remain).

Inheritance ("is-a"): Extends classes, but creates tight coupling. Composition is preferred over inheritance because it keeps components loosely coupled and allows changing behaviors dynamically at runtime.

CompositionDemo.java 
class Engine { void start() {} }

class Car {
    private final Engine engine; // Composition: Car owns Engine lifecycle

    public Car() {
        this.engine = new Engine();
    }
    
    public void drive() { engine.start(); }
}

What’s the role of the Mediator pattern?

Mediator Pattern: Restricts direct communications between objects, forcing them to collaborate solely through a mediator object, reducing direct dependencies.

Decoupling: Facilitates loose coupling: objects do not need to know the structure or references of other components.

Enterprise Usage: Spring's `ApplicationEventPublisher` acts as a mediator, routing events to listeners without direct publisher-subscriber coupling.

MediatorDemo.java 
import java.util.*;

class ChatMediator {
    private final List<User> users = new ArrayList<>();
    public void addUser(User u) { users.add(u); }
    public void broadcast(String msg, User sender) {
        for (User u : users) {
            if (u != sender) u.receive(msg);
        }
    }
}
abstract class User {
    protected ChatMediator mediator;
    public User(ChatMediator m) { this.mediator = m; }
    public abstract void receive(String m);
}

What’s the difference between BFS and DFS?

BFS (Breadth-First Search): Traverses a graph level-by-level, utilizing a FIFO Queue. It explores all neighbors of a node before moving to the next level, making it ideal for finding the shortest path in unweighted graphs.

DFS (Depth-First Search): Traverses along path branches as deep as possible before backtracking, using recursion or a LIFO Stack. It is commonly used for topological sorting or cycle detection.

Complexity: Both algorithms visit all vertices (V) and edges (E), executing in O(V + E) complexity.

GraphSearch.java 
import java.util.*;

public class GraphSearch {
    public void bfs(List<List<Integer>> adj, int start, int vertices) {
        boolean[] visited = new boolean[vertices];
        Queue<Integer> queue = new LinkedList<>();

        visited[start] = true;
        queue.offer(start);

        while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
            int node = queue.poll();
            System.out.print(node + " ");
            for (int neighbor : adj.get(node)) {
                if (!visited[neighbor]) {
                    visited[neighbor] = true;
                    queue.offer(neighbor);
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

Difference between Stack and Queue?

Stack (LIFO): Last-In, First-Out collection. Elements are pushed onto the top and popped off the top (e.g. tracking nested execution frames or undo history).

Queue (FIFO): First-In, First-Out collection. Elements are offered at the tail and polled from the head (e.g. task scheduling, messaging queues).

Deque Interface: In modern Java, avoid the legacy Stack class (which is synchronized). Use ArrayDeque, which implements the Deque interface and behaves as both a stack and a queue.

DequeUsage.java 
import java.util.ArrayDeque;
import java.util.Deque;

public class DequeUsage {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Stack Behavior (LIFO)
        Deque<String> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
        stack.push("Frame1");
        stack.push("Frame2");
        System.out.println("Pop: " + stack.pop()); // Frame2

        // Queue Behavior (FIFO)
        Deque<String> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        queue.offer("Task1");
        queue.offer("Task2");
        System.out.println("Poll: " + queue.poll()); // Task1
    }
}

What’s the difference between Array and LinkedList?

Overview: Array: fixed size, fast access. LinkedList: dynamic, fast insert/delete.

Enterprise Standard: In production environments, this behavior must be carefully tested, integrated into CI/CD pipelines, and monitored via standard observability tools.

Best Practice: Keep configurations modular, optimize resource utilization, handle exceptions gracefully, and implement proper logging/metrics trackers.

EnterpriseSnippet.java 
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

public class EnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(EnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void executeLogic() {
        log.info("Processing business flow using standard patterns...");
    }
}

How do you ensure consistency in distributed DBs?

Distributed Transactions: In microservices, maintaining ACID consistency across multiple databases is challenging. Traditional Two-Phase Commit (2PC) blocks threads and reduces availability.

Saga Pattern: A distributed transaction pattern that coordinates a sequence of local transactions. Each service updates its local database and publishes an event that triggers the next step.

Compensating Transactions: If a step in the Saga fails, the system executes backward-compensating transactions to roll back updates made in previous steps, ensuring eventual consistency.

SagaOrchestration.java 
public class SagaOrchestration {
    public void processOrderFlow() {
        try {
            paymentService.charge();
            inventoryService.deduct();
            shippingService.ship();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            // Trigger compensating rollbacks
            paymentService.refund();
            inventoryService.restore();
        }
    }
}

What are the different deployment options for a Spring Boot application?

Zero-Downtime Deployment: Deploy updates without service interruption. Common strategies include Rolling Updates, Blue-Green deployments, and Canary releases.

Blue-Green: Run two identical environments: Blue (active production) and Green (new release). Route traffic to Green after it passes integration testing, allowing instant rollback on failure.

Kubernetes Integration: Configure rolling updates and liveness/readiness probes to coordinate pod recycling safely.

DeploymentProbes.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: order-service
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: order-app
        image: order-service:latest
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /actuator/health/readiness
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 20
          periodSeconds: 10

How can you implement distributed session management in a Spring Boot application using Spring Session?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

Differentiate between Spring MVC and Spring Boot.

DispatcherServlet: The central Front Controller. It intercepts all incoming HTTP requests and delegates processing to HandlerMappings.

HandlerMapping & Controller: HandlerMapping identifies the correct `@Controller` method matching the request path. The controller executes business logic, returning data or a view name.

ViewResolver: Resolves view names (like JSP or Thymeleaf templates) and renders the response. For REST endpoints, `@RestController` writes JSON directly to the HTTP response body using HttpMessageConverters.

RestMvcController.java 
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.GetMapping;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RestController;

@RestController // Combines @Controller and @ResponseBody
public class RestMvcController {
    
    @GetMapping("/api/status")
    public Status getStatus() {
        return new Status("ACTIVE"); // Auto-serialized to JSON via Jackson
    }
}
class Status {
    private String val;
    public Status(String v) { this.val = v; }
    public String getVal() { return val; }
}

What is the role of the @RestController annotation in Spring Boot?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

Explain the concept of dependency injection in Spring Boot.

Inversion of Control (IoC): Delegates control of bean configuration, instantiation, and dependency wiring to an external container (Spring IoC Container).

Dependency Injection (DI): The pattern used to implement IoC. Instead of objects instantiating dependencies directly, Spring supplies dependencies via constructor, setter, or field injection.

Best Practice: Constructor injection is preferred because it enforces immutability, facilitates unit testing using mock objects, and prevents partial bean construction bugs.

ConstructorInjection.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

interface Engine { void run(); }

@Component
public class ConstructorInjection {
    private final Engine engine; // Immutable final field

    // Constructor injection (Autowired is optional since Spring 4.3)
    public ConstructorInjection(Engine engine) {
        this.engine = engine;
    }
}

Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }

What is the role of the embedded servlet container in Spring Boot?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

Describe the purpose and usage of the @Conditional annotation in Spring Boot.

Conditional Bean Registration: Spring provides the `@Conditional` annotation to register beans dynamically in the application context based on specific runtime conditions.

Common Variations: Spring Boot defines multiple derived annotations like `@ConditionalOnProperty` (checks configuration properties), `@ConditionalOnClass` (checks classpath files), and `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` (registers a bean only if no other bean of the same type exists).

ConditionalConfig.java 
import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.condition.ConditionalOnProperty;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration;

@Configuration
public class ConditionalConfig {
    @Bean
    // Only registered if 'feature.audit.enabled' is set to true in properties
    @ConditionalOnProperty(name = "feature.audit.enabled", havingValue = "true")
    public AuditService auditService() {
        return new AuditService();
    }
}
class AuditService {}

Explain the concept of Spring Boot's actuator health checks and customizing health indicators.

Spring Boot Actuator: Provides production-ready monitoring and management endpoints. Key endpoints include: `/actuator/health` (liveness/readiness indicators), `/actuator/metrics` (JVM, memory, request rates), `/actuator/env` (loaded properties), and `/actuator/loggers` (dynamic logging level overrides).

Configuration: By default, only `/actuator/health` is exposed. You must enable other endpoints explicitly in configuration properties.

application.properties 
# Expose Prometheus metrics and health check endpoints
management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info,prometheus,metrics
management.endpoint.health.show-details=always

How can you implement distributed caching in a Spring Boot application using Hazelcast or Redis?

What is Spring Boot, and how does it differ from Spring Framework?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

Explain the difference between @Component, @Repository, @Service, and @Controller annotations in Spring Boot.

Stereotype Annotations: Annotations that mark classes as Spring-managed beans, registering them in the application context during component scans.

@Component: The generic root stereotype annotation indicating a class is a Spring bean.

@Service: Used on service classes, indicating business transaction components.

@Repository: Used on data access components, indicating integration with database storage and automatically translating raw SQL exceptions into Spring's unified `DataAccessException` hierarchy.

StereotypeBean.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Repository;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;

@Repository
class UserRepository {
    // Data access logic...
}

@Service
public class StereotypeBean {
    private final UserRepository repo;
    public StereotypeBean(UserRepository r) { this.repo = r; }
}

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

What Is Dependency Injection?

Inversion of Control (IoC): Delegates control of bean configuration, instantiation, and dependency wiring to an external container (Spring IoC Container).

Dependency Injection (DI): The pattern used to implement IoC. Instead of objects instantiating dependencies directly, Spring supplies dependencies via constructor, setter, or field injection.

Best Practice: Constructor injection is preferred because it enforces immutability, facilitates unit testing using mock objects, and prevents partial bean construction bugs.

ConstructorInjection.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

interface Engine { void run(); }

@Component
public class ConstructorInjection {
    private final Engine engine; // Immutable final field

    // Constructor injection (Autowired is optional since Spring 4.3)
    public ConstructorInjection(Engine engine) {
        this.engine = engine;
    }
}

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Which Is the Best Way of Injecting Beans and Why?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

What Is the Difference Between BeanFactory and ApplicationContext?

Factory Method: Defines an interface or abstract class for creating objects, but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate, encapsulating object creation logic.

Abstract Factory: Provides an interface for creating families of related or dependent objects without specifying their concrete classes.

Selection Criteria: Use Factory Method for creating single objects of a polymorphic type. Switch to Abstract Factory when you need to coordinate multiple related polymorphic objects (like UI button and panel styling families).

FactoryDemo.java 
interface Shape { void draw(); }
class Circle implements Shape { @Override public void draw() { System.out.println("Circle"); } }
class Square implements Shape { @Override public void draw() { System.out.println("Square"); } }

class ShapeFactory {
    public static Shape getShape(String type) {
        if ("CIRCLE".equalsIgnoreCase(type)) return new Circle();
        if ("SQUARE".equalsIgnoreCase(type)) return new Square();
        throw new IllegalArgumentException("Unknown shape: " + type);
    }
}

Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    }

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.
