How to Define the Scope of a Bean?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

Are Singleton Beans Thread-Safe?

Singleton Pattern: Ensures a class has only one instance per JVM classloader and provides a global access point to it.

Double-Checked Locking: Uses a synchronized block inside a null check to initialize the instance lazily. The instance variable must be marked volatile to prevent instruction reordering bugs.

Bill Pugh Holder: The preferred lazy-initialization approach. It leverages a private static inner helper class, which is loaded only when getInstance() is invoked, guaranteeing thread-safety without synchronization overhead.

BillPughSingleton.java 
public class BillPughSingleton {
    private BillPughSingleton() {} // Prevent direct instantiation

    private static class SingletonHelper {
        private static final BillPughSingleton INSTANCE = new BillPughSingleton();
    }

    public static BillPughSingleton getInstance() {
        return SingletonHelper.INSTANCE; // Lazy loaded and thread-safe
    }
}

What Does the Spring Bean Life Cycle Look Like?

Cycle Detection: In directed graphs, a cycle exists if there is a back-edge during traversal. This is resolved using a Depth-First Search (DFS) tracking vertex visiting states.

Three-State Coloring: Mark nodes with states: (1) Unvisited: not yet processed; (2) Visiting: currently in the active DFS recursion stack; (3) Visited: fully processed.

Cycle Identification: If during DFS traversal of a node's neighbors we encounter a neighbor marked as **Visiting**, a back-edge is present, indicating a cycle.

CycleDetector.java 
import java.util.*;

public class CycleDetector {
    public boolean hasCycle(int v, List<List<Integer>> adj) {
        int[] visited = new int[v]; // 0: unvisited, 1: visiting, 2: visited
        for (int i = 0; i < v; i++) {
            if (visited[i] == 0) {
                if (dfs(i, adj, visited)) return true;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }

    private boolean dfs(int node, List<List<Integer>> adj, int[] visited) {
        visited[node] = 1; // Mark as visiting
        for (int neighbor : adj.get(node)) {
            if (visited[neighbor] == 1) return true; // Cycle detected
            if (visited[neighbor] == 0) {
                if (dfs(neighbor, adj, visited)) return true;
            }
        }
        visited[node] = 2; // Mark as fully visited
        return false;
    }
}

How Does the Scope Prototype Work?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

How to Get ServletContext and ServletConfig Objects in a Spring Bean?

ServletContext: A servlet container context representing the entire web application. It is shared across all servlets and filters, allowing them to share resources, log messages, and access web assets.

ServletConfig: Represents parameters specific to a single servlet instance. It is loaded during servlet initialization.

Spring Integration: Spring beans in a web context can access both objects by implementing `ServletContextAware` or `ServletConfigAware`, or by autowiring them directly.

ServletAwareBean.java 
import jakarta.servlet.ServletContext;
import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Autowired;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Component
public class ServletAwareBean {
    @Autowired
    private ServletContext servletContext; // Direct injection of ServletContext

    public void logAppPath() {
        System.out.println("Context Path: " + servletContext.getContextPath());
    }
}

What Is a Controller in Spring MVC?

DispatcherServlet: The central Front Controller. It intercepts all incoming HTTP requests and delegates processing to HandlerMappings.

HandlerMapping & Controller: HandlerMapping identifies the correct `@Controller` method matching the request path. The controller executes business logic, returning data or a view name.

ViewResolver: Resolves view names (like JSP or Thymeleaf templates) and renders the response. For REST endpoints, `@RestController` writes JSON directly to the HTTP response body using HttpMessageConverters.

RestMvcController.java 
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.GetMapping;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RestController;

@RestController // Combines @Controller and @ResponseBody
public class RestMvcController {
    
    @GetMapping("/api/status")
    public Status getStatus() {
        return new Status("ACTIVE"); // Auto-serialized to JSON via Jackson
    }
}
class Status {
    private String val;
    public Status(String v) { this.val = v; }
    public String getVal() { return val; }
}

How Does the @RequestMapping Annotation Work?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

What Is Aspect-Oriented Programming?

AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming) complements OOP by allowing developers to modularize cross-cutting concerns (e.g., logging, transaction management, caching, security) that slice across multiple business classes.

Key AOP Terminologies:
  • Aspect: The module containing the cross-cutting concern (e.g., a `TransactionAspect` class). Contains Pointcuts and Advices.
  • JoinPoint: A point during execution of the program (such as method execution or exception handling) where an aspect can be plugged in. In Spring AOP, a JoinPoint is **always method execution**.
  • Pointcut: A predicate expression matching JoinPoints. It defines *where* the advice should run (e.g., execution of any method in `com.service.*`).
  • Advice: Action taken by an aspect at a particular JoinPoint. Types: `@Before`, `@After`, `@AfterReturning`, `@AfterThrowing`, and `@Around` (most powerful: can block execution, modify return values, and handle exceptions).
Types of Weaving (Plugging aspects into application types):
  1. Compile-Time Weaving: Aspects are woven into Java bytecode at compilation using the AspectJ compiler (`ajc`). Has zero runtime performance overhead.
  2. Post-Compile Weaving (Binary Weaving): Woven into existing compiled `.class` or `.jar` files.
  3. Load-Time Weaving (LTW): Woven dynamically when classes are loaded into JVM memory by using a JVM Java Agent (`-javaagent:aspectjweaver.jar`).
  4. Runtime Weaving (Spring AOP Default): Uses dynamic proxies (JDK dynamic proxies for interfaces, CGLIB subclasses for concrete classes) to intercept method calls at execution time. Limited to public method calls initiated outside the bean.

What Is Weaving?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}
Framework & Errors

Spring Boot & Exceptions

Auto-configuration internals, custom Spring Boot starters, profiles, externalized configuration, actuator health monitoring, and global exception handling with @ControllerAdvice and custom error pages.

What is your systematic process to handle bugs in a production microservice environment? Show how to correlate logs using MDC.

Systematic Process: (1) Catch alerts (e.g. Dynatrace, Sentry), (2) Correlate logs via Trace ID using ELK/Splunk, (3) Extract inputs to write a failing integration test, (4) Apply fix, verify via CI/CD, and monitor post-deployment metrics.

MDC (Mapped Diagnostic Context): MDC allows placing context variables (like traceId, userId) in a thread-local map, automatically printing them in every log statement.

Context Propagation: When calling external APIs, propagate this trace context in HTTP headers (e.g. X-B3-TraceId or W3C Trace Context) to maintain end-to-end trace visibility.

Resolution Time: This prevents developers from manually parsing disparate logs, shortening production outage resolution times.

LoggingFilter.java 
import jakarta.servlet.*;
import jakarta.servlet.http.HttpServletRequest;
import org.slf4j.MDC;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import java.io.IOException;
import java.util.UUID;

@Component
public class LoggingFilter implements Filter {
    private static final String TRACE_ID_KEY = "traceId";

    @Override
    public void doFilter(ServletRequest request, ServletResponse response, FilterChain chain)
            throws IOException, ServletException {
        HttpServletRequest httpRequest = (HttpServletRequest) request;
        
        // Extract traceId from incoming headers, or generate one if absent
        String traceId = httpRequest.getHeader("X-Correlation-ID");
        if (traceId == null || traceId.isEmpty()) {
            traceId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();
        }

        // Bind traceId to thread-local MDC context
        MDC.put(TRACE_ID_KEY, traceId);

        try {
            chain.doFilter(request, response);
        } finally {
            MDC.remove(TRACE_ID_KEY); // Clean context to prevent thread leak
        }
    }
}

How does Spring Boot handle JSON parsing? Show how to customize the ObjectMapper programmatically.

Jackson Integration: Spring Boot auto-configures the Jackson library's ObjectMapper instance to serialize and deserialize HTTP request/response payloads.

Programmatic Customization: Customize serialization behavior by defining a Jackson2ObjectMapperBuilder bean or registering custom serializer modules.

Common Settings: Common customizations include formatting dates (Java 8 Date/Time API), ignoring null properties, or converting CamelCase keys to SnakeCase.

Custom Deserializers: Use custom deserializers to validate incoming payload constraints or scrub raw input data before it hits the application layers.

JacksonCustomizerConfig.java 
import com.fasterxml.jackson.annotation.JsonInclude;
import com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.ObjectMapper;
import com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.SerializationFeature;
import com.fasterxml.jackson.datatype.jsr310.JavaTimeModule;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Primary;

@Configuration
public class JacksonCustomizerConfig {

    @Bean
    @Primary
    public ObjectMapper customObjectMapper() {
        ObjectMapper mapper = new ObjectMapper();
        
        // 1. Support Java 8 Date/Time types
        mapper.registerModule(new JavaTimeModule());
        mapper.disable(SerializationFeature.WRITE_DATES_AS_TIMESTAMPS);
        
        // 2. Exclude null fields from JSON output payload to save bandwidth
        mapper.setSerializationInclusion(JsonInclude.Include.NON_NULL);
        
        // 3. Prevent failure on unknown properties in JSON payload
        mapper.configure(com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.DeserializationFeature.FAIL_ON_UNKNOWN_PROPERTIES, false);
        
        return mapper;
    }
}

Explain Mock annotations in Spring Boot: Compare @Mock vs @MockBean, and @Spy vs @SpyBean.

@Mock: Provided by Mockito. It creates a mock instance of a class for plain unit tests. It does not interact with the Spring context, keeping tests fast.

@MockBean: Provided by Spring Boot. It creates a mock and registers it inside the Spring ApplicationContext, replacing any existing bean of that type for integration testing.

@Spy: Creates a partial mock where real methods are called unless explicitly stubbed, used in standard Mockito test suites.

@SpyBean: Wraps an existing Spring context bean in a spy wrapper, allowing you to track method invocations or stub selective methods during integration tests.

SpringIntegrationTest.java 
import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Autowired;
import org.springframework.boot.test.autoconfigure.web.servlet.WebMvcTest;
import org.springframework.boot.test.mock.mockito.MockBean;
import org.springframework.test.web.servlet.MockMvc;
import static org.mockito.Mockito.when;
import static org.springframework.test.web.servlet.request.MockMvcRequestBuilders.get;
import static org.springframework.test.web.servlet.result.MockMvcResultMatchers.status;

@WebMvcTest(UserController.class)
public class SpringIntegrationTest {

    @Autowired
    private MockMvc mockMvc;

    // Registers mock instance in ApplicationContext, replacing the real bean
    @MockBean
    private UserService userService;

    @Test
    public void testGetUserEndpoint() throws Exception {
        // Stub mock behavior
        when(userService.getUserName(1L)).thenReturn("Birat");

        mockMvc.perform(get("/users/1"))
               .andExpect(status().isOk());
    }
}

// Controller under test
class UserController {
    private final UserService service;
    public UserController(UserService service) { this.service = service; }
}
interface UserService { String getUserName(Long id); }

Explain global exception handling in Spring Boot. How do you implement @ControllerAdvice and custom error pages?

Spring MVC provides centralized exception handling to separate error-handling boilerplate from business controllers.

  • @ControllerAdvice / @RestControllerAdvice: Intercepts exceptions thrown by any `@Controller` or `@RestController` in the application.
  • @ExceptionHandler: Placed on advice methods, defining which exception class to handle. The method returns standard HTTP response payloads (`ResponseEntity`) and custom status codes.
  • Custom Error Pages: If an exception escapes controllers and bubbles up to the servlet container, Spring Boot's `BasicErrorController` handles it. By default, it routes to `/error`. You can customize this by creating an HTML file at `/src/main/resources/templates/error/404.html` or `/src/main/resources/public/error/5xx.html`. Spring Boot automatically maps error codes to these specific static or dynamic templates.

GlobalExceptionHandler.java 
import org.springframework.http.HttpStatus;
import org.springframework.http.ResponseEntity;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.ExceptionHandler;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RestControllerAdvice;
import java.time.LocalDateTime;

// Custom Error DTO
record ErrorResponse(String message, HttpStatus status, LocalDateTime timestamp) {}

// Custom Business Exception
class ResourceNotFoundException extends RuntimeException {
    public ResourceNotFoundException(String message) { super(message); }
}

@RestControllerAdvice
public class GlobalExceptionHandler {

    @ExceptionHandler(ResourceNotFoundException.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleNotFound(ResourceNotFoundException ex) {
        ErrorResponse err = new ErrorResponse(ex.getMessage(), HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND, LocalDateTime.now());
        return new ResponseEntity<>(err, HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND);
    }

    @ExceptionHandler(Exception.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleGeneralException(Exception ex) {
        ErrorResponse err = new ErrorResponse("An internal server error occurred.", HttpStatus.INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR, LocalDateTime.now());
        return new ResponseEntity<>(err, HttpStatus.INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR);
    }
}

How does Spring Boot auto-configuration and starters work internally? Provide a complete custom starter auto-configuration.

Spring Boot Starters are dependency aggregators. Adding `spring-boot-starter-web` imports Tomcat, Jackson, validation, and Spring MVC libraries under a single dependency block, ensuring version compatibility.

Auto-Configuration Internals: At startup, Spring Boot scans files named `META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports` (Spring Boot 3.x) or `META-INF/spring.factories` (older versions) located inside starter jars on the classpath. This file contains a list of fully-qualified `@Configuration` class names. The container processes these configurations conditionally using `@Conditional` annotations:

  • @ConditionalOnClass: Activate only if specific classes are present on the classpath.
  • @ConditionalOnMissingBean: Register a bean only if the user hasn't defined their own custom bean override.
  • @ConditionalOnProperty: Activate only if a configuration property matches expected values.

CustomTracerAutoConfiguration.java 
import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration;
import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.condition.ConditionalOnClass;
import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.condition.ConditionalOnMissingBean;
import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.condition.ConditionalOnProperty;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean;

// Mock custom tracing library bean
class Tracer {
    private final String prefix;
    public Tracer(String prefix) { this.prefix = prefix; }
    public void trace(String message) { System.out.println(prefix + " -> " + message); }
}

@AutoConfiguration
@ConditionalOnClass(Tracer.class)
@ConditionalOnProperty(prefix = "custom.tracer", name = "enabled", havingValue = "true", matchIfMissing = true)
public class CustomTracerAutoConfiguration {

    @Bean
    @ConditionalOnMissingBean
    public Tracer defaultTracer() {
        return new Tracer("[DEFAULT-APP-TRACE]");
    }
}

How does Spring Boot auto-configuration work?

Auto-Configuration: Spring Boot automatically configures beans in the application context based on classpath dependencies and properties.

Mechanism: Enabled by `@EnableAutoConfiguration` (part of `@SpringBootApplication`). It reads configuration targets from `org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports` in Spring Boot 3.x.

Conditional Loading: Auto-configuration classes utilize conditional annotations (like `@ConditionalOnClass`, `@ConditionalOnProperty`) to load beans only when their prerequisites are met.

AutoConfigDemo.java 
import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.condition.ConditionalOnClass;
import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.condition.ConditionalOnProperty;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration;

@Configuration
@ConditionalOnClass(name = "com.zaxxer.hikari.HikariDataSource")
public class AutoConfigDemo {
    @Bean
    @ConditionalOnProperty(name = "app.db.enabled", havingValue = "true")
    public DatabaseService customDbService() {
        return new DatabaseService();
    }
}
class DatabaseService {}

How to handle circular dependencies?

Circular Dependency: A dependency bug where Bean A requires Bean B, while Bean B simultaneously requires Bean A. This prevents Spring from fully instantiating the beans, causing startup crashes.

Resolution Strategies: (1) **Constructor Refactoring:** Redesign the classes to eliminate the circular dependency. (2) **@Lazy annotation:** Annotate the constructor parameter with `@Lazy` to inject a dynamic proxy instead of the fully instantiated bean at startup. (3) **Setter Injection:** Use setter injection instead of constructor injection.

CircularResolution.java 
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Lazy;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Component
class BeanA {
    private final BeanB beanB;
    // @Lazy resolves circular dependency by injecting a proxy at startup
    public BeanA(@Lazy BeanB beanB) {
        this.beanB = beanB;
    }
}

@Component
class BeanB {
    private final BeanA beanA;
    public BeanB(BeanA beanA) { this.beanA = beanA; }
}

How do you implement inter-service communication?

Communication Options: Microservices can communicate: (1) **Synchronously:** via HTTP (using RestTemplate, WebClient, or OpenFeign) or gRPC. (2) **Asynchronously:** via message brokers (Kafka or RabbitMQ).

OpenFeign Client: A declarative REST client. Developers declare interface signatures annotated with Spring MVC mapping annotations, and Feign generates proxy implementations dynamically at runtime.

UserFeignClient.java 
import org.springframework.cloud.openfeign.FeignClient;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.GetMapping;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.PathVariable;

// OpenFeign client integrates with Eureka load balancer
@FeignClient(name = "USER-SERVICE", path = "/api/users")
public interface UserFeignClient {
    @GetMapping("/{id}")
    UserDto getUserById(@PathVariable("id") Long id);
}
class UserDto {}

How do you ensure backward compatibility between services?

API Versioning: Maintain backward compatibility by versioning endpoints (e.g. `/api/v1/users` vs `/api/v2/users`) or propagating versions in HTTP request headers.

Schema Evolution: Ensure database schemas and message payloads are backward-compatible. Add optional columns/fields, define fallback default values, and avoid deleting existing columns.

VersionedController.java 
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.GetMapping;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RestController;

@RestController
public class VersionedController {
    @GetMapping("/api/v1/data")
    public String getDataV1() { return "Legacy format"; }

    @GetMapping("/api/v2/data")
    public String getDataV2() { return "New extended format"; }
}

How does actuator help in production?

Spring Boot Actuator: Provides production-ready monitoring and management endpoints. Key endpoints include: `/actuator/health` (liveness/readiness indicators), `/actuator/metrics` (JVM, memory, request rates), `/actuator/env` (loaded properties), and `/actuator/loggers` (dynamic logging level overrides).

Configuration: By default, only `/actuator/health` is exposed. You must enable other endpoints explicitly in configuration properties.

application.properties 
# Expose Prometheus metrics and health check endpoints
management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info,prometheus,metrics
management.endpoint.health.show-details=always

What tools do you use for logs and metrics?

Centralized Logging: Aggregate container logs (stdout/stderr) into central indexing systems like ELK (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) or Splunk.

Distributed Tracing: OpenTelemetry or Micrometer Tracing assign unique Trace IDs to requests. The Trace ID is propagated across microservice boundaries in HTTP headers, tracking request flows end-to-end.

MDC Logs: Use Mapped Diagnostic Context (MDC) to bind Trace IDs to threads, printing trace metadata automatically in log messages.

MdcTraceFilter.java 
import jakarta.servlet.*;
import jakarta.servlet.http.HttpServletRequest;
import org.slf4j.MDC;
import java.io.IOException;
import java.util.UUID;

public class MdcTraceFilter implements Filter {
    @Override
    public void doFilter(ServletRequest req, ServletResponse res, FilterChain c) throws IOException, ServletException {
        HttpServletRequest httpReq = (HttpServletRequest) req;
        String traceId = httpReq.getHeader("X-Correlation-ID");
        if (traceId == null) traceId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();
        
        MDC.put("traceId", traceId); // Bind Trace ID to thread logging context
        try {
            c.doFilter(req, res);
        } finally {
            MDC.clear(); // CRITICAL: Prevent thread pool leaks
        }
    }
}

How do you achieve zero-downtime deployment?

Zero-Downtime Deployment: Deploy updates without service interruption. Common strategies include Rolling Updates, Blue-Green deployments, and Canary releases.

Blue-Green: Run two identical environments: Blue (active production) and Green (new release). Route traffic to Green after it passes integration testing, allowing instant rollback on failure.

Kubernetes Integration: Configure rolling updates and liveness/readiness probes to coordinate pod recycling safely.

DeploymentProbes.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: order-service
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: order-app
        image: order-service:latest
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /actuator/health/readiness
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 20
          periodSeconds: 10

What is blue-green deployment?

Zero-Downtime Deployment: Deploy updates without service interruption. Common strategies include Rolling Updates, Blue-Green deployments, and Canary releases.

Blue-Green: Run two identical environments: Blue (active production) and Green (new release). Route traffic to Green after it passes integration testing, allowing instant rollback on failure.

Kubernetes Integration: Configure rolling updates and liveness/readiness probes to coordinate pod recycling safely.

DeploymentProbes.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: order-service
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: order-app
        image: order-service:latest
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /actuator/health/readiness
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 20
          periodSeconds: 10

What happens if a service crashes? How do you recover?

Overview: Retry, circuit break, autoscale, use Kubernetes liveness probes.

Enterprise Standard: In production environments, this behavior must be carefully tested, integrated into CI/CD pipelines, and monitored via standard observability tools.

Best Practice: Keep configurations modular, optimize resource utilization, handle exceptions gracefully, and implement proper logging/metrics trackers.

EnterpriseSnippet.java 
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

public class EnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(EnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void executeLogic() {
        log.info("Processing business flow using standard patterns...");
    }
}

How is Lazy Initialization useful in patterns?

Lazy Initialization: Delays object creation until it is accessed for the first time, saving memory resources and improving application startup latency.

Spring @Lazy: Spring beans are initialized eagerly at startup by default. Annotating a bean or injection point with `@Lazy` defers creation until the bean is requested.

LazyLoader.java 
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Lazy;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Component
@Lazy // Instantiated only when first injected/called
public class LazyLoader {
    public LazyLoader() { System.out.println("Lazy bean initialized!"); }
}

How to profile slow queries?

Query Profiling: Slow queries degrade application performance and saturate database connections. Identify bottlenecks by analyzing query logs or checking the database slow query log.

EXPLAIN Command: Prepended to SELECT statements to output the database execution plan, showing whether the engine uses index scans or executes slow sequential scans.

Optimization: Add appropriate indexes, rewrite queries to eliminate wildcard SELECT *, use JOIN FETCH in JPA to prevent N+1 queries, and configure connection pool limits.

QueryProfiling.sql 
-- 1. Profile query plan to inspect scan behaviors
EXPLAIN ANALYZE 
SELECT * FROM orders 
WHERE customer_id = 4920 AND status = 'COMPLETED';

-- 2. Add composite index to optimize lookup
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_customer_status ON orders(customer_id, status);

How can you create a Spring Boot application using Gradle?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Component

    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

Describe the process of creating a RESTful API using Spring Boot.

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

How does Spring Boot integrate with messaging systems such as RabbitMQ?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;

@Component

How can you handle large file uploads in a Spring Boot application?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

What is the purpose of Spring Boot Actuator endpoints?

Spring Boot Actuator: Provides production-ready monitoring and management endpoints. Key endpoints include: `/actuator/health` (liveness/readiness indicators), `/actuator/metrics` (JVM, memory, request rates), `/actuator/env` (loaded properties), and `/actuator/loggers` (dynamic logging level overrides).

Configuration: By default, only `/actuator/health` is exposed. You must enable other endpoints explicitly in configuration properties.

application.properties 
# Expose Prometheus metrics and health check endpoints
management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info,prometheus,metrics
management.endpoint.health.show-details=always

Explain the concept of Spring Boot Actuator metrics and monitoring.

Spring Boot Actuator: Provides production-ready monitoring and management endpoints. Key endpoints include: `/actuator/health` (liveness/readiness indicators), `/actuator/metrics` (JVM, memory, request rates), `/actuator/env` (loaded properties), and `/actuator/loggers` (dynamic logging level overrides).

Configuration: By default, only `/actuator/health` is exposed. You must enable other endpoints explicitly in configuration properties.

application.properties 
# Expose Prometheus metrics and health check endpoints
management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info,prometheus,metrics
management.endpoint.health.show-details=always

What is the purpose of Spring Boot DevTools and how does it enhance development productivity?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

How can you implement asynchronous processing in a Spring Boot application?

Synchronous: The calling thread blocks, waiting for a response from the downstream service (e.g. RestTemplate HTTP calls). This wastes resources and can cause thread pool starvation under load.

Asynchronous: The calling thread returns immediately, executing operations non-blockingly via callbacks or future events (e.g. using `CompletableFuture` or reactive Streams).

AsyncExecutor.java 
import org.springframework.scheduling.annotation.Async;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;
import java.util.concurrent.CompletableFuture;

@Service
public class AsyncExecutor {
    @Async // Executes asynchronously on a separate thread pool
    public CompletableFuture<String> executeTask() {
        // Long running task...
        return CompletableFuture.completedFuture("Task Result");
    }
}

Describe the purpose and usage of the @Async annotation in Spring Boot.

Async side effects: Async functions return a Promise. The `useEffect` hook callback cannot be declared as `async` because it must return either nothing or a synchronous cleanup function.

Safe Execution: To execute async operations inside effects, declare a nested async function inside the effect and call it immediately.

Race conditions: Network responses can arrive out of order or after a component has unmounted. Resolve this by maintaining an active flag or aborting requests using an `AbortController` inside the cleanup function.

SafeAsyncEffect.jsx 
import React, { useState, useEffect } from 'react';

export default function SafeAsyncEffect({ itemId }) {
    const [data, setData] = useState(null);

    useEffect(() => {
        let active = true;
        const controller = new AbortController();

        async function loadData() {
            try {
                const res = await fetch(`/api/items/${itemId}`, { signal: controller.signal });
                const json = await res.json();
                if (active) setData(json);
            } catch (err) {
                if (err.name !== 'AbortError') console.error(err);
            }
        }

        loadData();

        return () => {
            active = false;
            controller.abort(); // Cleanup: abort network request
        };
    }, [itemId]);

    return <div>Item: {data?.name}</div>;
}

What is the purpose of the @Retryable annotation in Spring Boot?

Circuit Breaker: Resilience4j intercepts failures in downstream calls. If failures exceed a set threshold, the breaker transitions to the `OPEN` state, immediately failing subsequent requests to protect the system.

Fallback: In the OPEN state, requests execute local fallback methods to return cached data or default payloads, preventing cascading failures across the system.

Retry: Automatically retries transient network operations with a set backoff delay before triggering circuit breaker state changes.

ResilientService.java 
import io.github.resilience4j.circuitbreaker.annotation.CircuitBreaker;
import io.github.resilience4j.retry.annotation.Retry;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;

@Service
public class ResilientService {
    @CircuitBreaker(name = "userClient", fallbackMethod = "fallbackUser")
    @Retry(name = "userClient")
    public String fetchUserDetails(String userId) {
        // Downstream REST request...
        throw new RuntimeException("Service Down");
    }

    public String fallbackUser(String userId, Throwable t) {
        return "Cached User Data (Fallback reason: " + t.getMessage() + ")";
    }
}

How can you enable the auto-configuration feature in Spring Boot?

Auto-Configuration: Spring Boot automatically configures beans in the application context based on classpath dependencies and properties.

Mechanism: Enabled by `@EnableAutoConfiguration` (part of `@SpringBootApplication`). It reads configuration targets from `org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports` in Spring Boot 3.x.

Conditional Loading: Auto-configuration classes utilize conditional annotations (like `@ConditionalOnClass`, `@ConditionalOnProperty`) to load beans only when their prerequisites are met.

AutoConfigDemo.java 
import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.condition.ConditionalOnClass;
import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.condition.ConditionalOnProperty;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration;

@Configuration
@ConditionalOnClass(name = "com.zaxxer.hikari.HikariDataSource")
public class AutoConfigDemo {
    @Bean
    @ConditionalOnProperty(name = "app.db.enabled", havingValue = "true")
    public DatabaseService customDbService() {
        return new DatabaseService();
    }
}
class DatabaseService {}

How does Spring Boot handle external configuration?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

What is the purpose of the application.properties (or application.yml) file?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

Describe the Spring Boot auto-configuration mechanism.

Auto-Configuration: Spring Boot automatically configures beans in the application context based on classpath dependencies and properties.

Mechanism: Enabled by `@EnableAutoConfiguration` (part of `@SpringBootApplication`). It reads configuration targets from `org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports` in Spring Boot 3.x.

Conditional Loading: Auto-configuration classes utilize conditional annotations (like `@ConditionalOnClass`, `@ConditionalOnProperty`) to load beans only when their prerequisites are met.

AutoConfigDemo.java 
import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.condition.ConditionalOnClass;
import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.condition.ConditionalOnProperty;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration;

@Configuration
@ConditionalOnClass(name = "com.zaxxer.hikari.HikariDataSource")
public class AutoConfigDemo {
    @Bean
    @ConditionalOnProperty(name = "app.db.enabled", havingValue = "true")
    public DatabaseService customDbService() {
        return new DatabaseService();
    }
}
class DatabaseService {}

What is the purpose of the @Component annotation in Spring Boot?

Stereotype Annotations: Annotations that mark classes as Spring-managed beans, registering them in the application context during component scans.

@Component: The generic root stereotype annotation indicating a class is a Spring bean.

@Service: Used on service classes, indicating business transaction components.

@Repository: Used on data access components, indicating integration with database storage and automatically translating raw SQL exceptions into Spring's unified `DataAccessException` hierarchy.

StereotypeBean.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Repository;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;

@Repository
class UserRepository {
    // Data access logic...
}

@Service
public class StereotypeBean {
    private final UserRepository repo;
    public StereotypeBean(UserRepository r) { this.repo = r; }
}

Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

Describe the role of the CommandLineRunner and ApplicationRunner interfaces in Spring Boot.

Command Pattern: Encapsulates a request as an object, decoupling the invoker from the receiver. It supports queuing requests, transaction logs, and undo/redo systems.

Encapsulated Action: The Command object exposes a single method (like `execute()`) and maintains references to the target receiver and input parameters.

CommandPatternDemo.java 
interface Command { void execute(); }
class Light { public void turnOn() { System.out.println("Light ON"); } }

class LightOnCommand implements Command {
    private final Light light;
    public LightOnCommand(Light l) { this.light = l; }
    @Override public void execute() { light.turnOn(); }
}

What Is Spring Boot?

Overview: Actuator exposes operational endpoints for monitoring and managing applications in production. Key endpoints include: /actuator/health (liveness/readiness indicators), /actuator/metrics (JVM, memory, request rates), /actuator/env (loaded properties), and /actuator/loggers (dynamic logging level overrides).

Enterprise Standard: In production environments, this behavior must be carefully tested, integrated into CI/CD pipelines, and monitored via standard observability tools.

Best Practice: Keep configurations modular, optimize resource utilization, handle exceptions gracefully, and implement proper logging/metrics trackers.

EnterpriseSnippet.java 
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

public class EnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(EnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void executeLogic() {
        log.info("Processing business flow using standard patterns...");
    }
}

if million of data transection need in rest api , one good practice to that.

Asynchronous Streams

Spring WebFlux & Reactive

Reactive programming principles, Mono and Flux, WebClient vs RestTemplate, R2DBC database access, backpressure handling, and Netty event loop non-blocking database scheduler bypass.

How do you stream data non-blockingly using WebClient and CompletableFuture in Spring WebFlux?

Reactive Streams: WebFlux handles streaming using reactive types (Mono, Flux) mapped to the text/event-stream media type.

Producers: Stream items asynchronously using reactive pipelines, preventing worker thread blocks.

Receivers: WebClient consumes streams non-blockingly using bodyToFlux(Data.class), processing elements as they arrive.

CompletableFuture Bridge: To bridge blocking CompletableFuture logic, use Mono.fromFuture() or Flux.create() to adapt async operations into reactive streams.

ReactiveStreamController.java 
import org.springframework.http.MediaType;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.GetMapping;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RestController;
import org.springframework.web.reactive.function.client.WebClient;
import reactor.core.publisher.Flux;
import reactor.core.publisher.Mono;
import java.time.Duration;
import java.util.concurrent.CompletableFuture;

@RestController
public class ReactiveStreamController {

    // 1. Producer Endpoint - Streams data as Server-Sent Events (SSE)
    @GetMapping(value = "/stream/events", produces = MediaType.TEXT_EVENT_STREAM_VALUE)
    public Flux<String> streamEvents() {
        return Flux.interval(Duration.ofSeconds(1))
                   .map(seq -> "Event Sequence: " + seq);
    }

    // 2. Bridge async Future to Reactive Mono
    public Mono<String> bridgeFuture() {
        CompletableFuture<String> future = CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> "Async Data");
        return Mono.fromFuture(future); // Safe non-blocking bridge
    }

    // 3. Receiver Service - Consumes SSE stream using WebClient
    public void consumeStream() {
        WebClient client = WebClient.create("http://localhost:8080");
        
        Flux<String> eventStream = client.get()
            .uri("/stream/events")
            .accept(MediaType.TEXT_EVENT_STREAM)
            .retrieve()
            .bodyToFlux(String.class);

        eventStream.subscribe(
            data -> System.out.println("Received: " + data),
            err -> System.err.println("Stream error: " + err),
            () -> System.out.println("Stream completed.")
        );
    }
}

Explain Reactive Streams backpressure. How does Spring WebFlux handle it, and what are Mono and Flux?

Backpressure is the mechanism in Reactive Streams that allows a **Consumer** to signal to a **Publisher** how much data it can process at any given moment. This prevents a fast publisher from overwhelming a slow consumer, avoiding memory exhaustion and out-of-memory errors.

In WebFlux, Reactor handles backpressure automatically through TCP flow control. If Netty's outbound write buffers are full, the consumer slows down its demand requests (`Subscription.request(n)`), signaling the upstream publisher to slow down or buffer data.

Mono vs Flux
  • Mono<T>: A Reactive Streams publisher that emits **0 or 1** element, then completes. Useful for single entities, updates, or void results.
  • Flux<T>: A Reactive Streams publisher that emits **0 to N** elements, then completes. Useful for streaming datasets, real-time events, or chunked outputs.
ReactiveController.java 
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.GetMapping;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RestController;
import reactor.core.publisher.Flux;
import reactor.core.publisher.Mono;
import java.time.Duration;

@RestController
public class ReactiveController {

    @GetMapping("/api/v1/ping")
    public Mono<String> ping() {
        return Mono.just("Pong"); // Emits 1 item
    }

    @GetMapping(value = "/api/v1/event-stream", produces = "text/event-stream")
    public Flux<Long> getEventStream() {
        // Emit count increments every 1 second indefinitely
        return Flux.interval(Duration.ofSeconds(1));
    }
}

How do you handle blocking database calls (e.g. JDBC) inside a Spring WebFlux application without starving the Netty Event Loop?

Spring WebFlux is built on a non-blocking reactive event-loop execution model (typically running on Netty). The Netty event loop runs on a very small number of threads (matching CPU cores). If a thread blocks inside the event loop (e.g., executing a synchronous JDBC query, calling a slow blocking REST API, or doing heavy cryptography), **the entire application freezes** because no other incoming requests can be polled.

Scheduler-Bypass Strategy:

To safely invoke blocking library code, isolate the execution using publishOn() to offload execution onto a dedicated thread pool (Schedulers.boundedElastic()). This keeps the main Netty Event Loop threads free to process non-blocking network I/O.

ReactiveService.java 
import reactor.core.publisher.Mono;
import reactor.core.scheduler.Schedulers;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;

@Service
public class ReactiveService {

    // Simulates legacy blocking repository call
    private String fetchFromBlockingDb(String id) {
        try { Thread.sleep(500); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        return "Data-from-DB-" + id;
    }

    public Mono<String> getReactiveData(String id) {
        return Mono.fromCallable(() -> fetchFromBlockingDb(id))
            // Offload the blocking DB call to boundedElastic scheduler pool
            .subscribeOn(Schedulers.boundedElastic()) 
            .map(data -> data + " | Processed on Non-blocking Pipeline");
    }
}

What Is Spring WebFlux?

Reactive Architecture: Spring WebFlux is a non-blocking reactive web framework running on Netty, bypassing the thread-per-request model.

Mono & Flux: Mono represents a reactive publisher that emits 0 or 1 item. Flux represents a publisher that emits a stream of 0 to N items.

Event Loop: Netty utilizes a small number of event loop threads to handle thousands of concurrent requests non-blockingly. Blocking operations (like legacy JDBC) must be offloaded to separate schedulers to prevent event loop starvation.

ReactiveController.java 
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.GetMapping;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RestController;
import reactor.core.publisher.Flux;
import java.time.Duration;

@RestController
public class ReactiveController {
    
    @GetMapping(value = "/api/stream", produces = "text/event-stream")
    public Flux<String> streamData() {
        return Flux.interval(Duration.ofSeconds(1))
                   .map(sec -> "Pulse: " + sec)
                   .take(10); // Safe non-blocking stream
    }
}

What Are the Mono and Flux Types?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

What Is the Use of WebClient and WebTestClient?

WebClient: A reactive, non-blocking HTTP client introduced in Spring 5 to replace the blocking RestTemplate. It supports synchronous, asynchronous, and streaming scenarios.

Non-Blocking Fetch: WebClient registers request callbacks and yields threads immediately. Threads are recycled while awaiting network responses, significantly improving concurrency.

WebClientDemo.java 
import org.springframework.web.reactive.function.client.WebClient;
import reactor.core.publisher.Mono;

public class WebClientDemo {
    private final WebClient webClient = WebClient.create("https://api.valore.com");

    public Mono<String> fetchUser(String id) {
        return webClient.get()
            .uri("/users/{id}", id)
            .retrieve()
            .bodyToMono(String.class); // Safe non-blocking execution
    }
}

Can We Use Both Web MVC and WebFlux in the Same Application?

DispatcherServlet: The central Front Controller. It intercepts all incoming HTTP requests and delegates processing to HandlerMappings.

HandlerMapping & Controller: HandlerMapping identifies the correct `@Controller` method matching the request path. The controller executes business logic, returning data or a view name.

ViewResolver: Resolves view names (like JSP or Thymeleaf templates) and renders the response. For REST endpoints, `@RestController` writes JSON directly to the HTTP response body using HttpMessageConverters.

RestMvcController.java 
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.GetMapping;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RestController;

@RestController // Combines @Controller and @ResponseBody
public class RestMvcController {
    
    @GetMapping("/api/status")
    public Status getStatus() {
        return new Status("ACTIVE"); // Auto-serialized to JSON via Jackson
    }
}
class Status {
    private String val;
    public Status(String v) { this.val = v; }
    public String getVal() { return val; }
}
Endpoints & Access

REST APIs & Security

RESTful controllers, JWT with Spring Security, OAuth2 protocols, Role-Based Access Control, CORS, rate limiting (Bucket4j/Redis), and processing millions of transactions synchronously/asynchronously.

If millions of data transactions need processing via a REST API, what are the best design practices?

Processing millions of records synchronously in a single HTTP request is an anti-pattern. It leads to timeouts, high memory usage, and blocks the client. Here are the correct production-grade approaches:

  1. Asynchronous Processing & Job Status Polling: The API accepts the payload, validates the schema, publishes the job to a message queue or database, and returns `202 Accepted` immediately with a status tracking URL. The client polls this URL (`/api/v1/jobs/{jobId}`) to check progress.
  2. Chunked Batch Processing (Spring Batch): Break the large load into manageable chunks (e.g., 500-1000 records). Read chunk -> Process/Validate -> Write. Use database-level bulk operations (like `EntityManager.flush()` and `clear()` to prevent memory exhaustion).
  3. Streaming with Kafka: Instead of a single large payload, stream records individually to a Kafka topic. A group of consumer services processes these records asynchronously at their own rate, providing natural backpressure and scaling.

BulkProcessingPattern.java 
import jakarta.persistence.EntityManager;
import org.springframework.transaction.annotation.Transactional;
import java.util.List;

public class BulkProcessingPattern {
    private final EntityManager entityManager;

    public BulkProcessingPattern(EntityManager em) { this.entityManager = em; }

    @Transactional
    public void executeBatchInsert(List<Object> largeCollection) {
        int batchSize = 500;
        for (int i = 0; i < largeCollection.size(); i++) {
            entityManager.persist(largeCollection.get(i));
            
            // Periodically flush updates to the DB and clear memory
            if (i > 0 && i % batchSize == 0) {
                entityManager.flush(); // Sends sql updates to database
                entityManager.clear(); // Evicts entity cache to prevent OutOfMemoryError
            }
        }
    }
}

How do you handle distributed payment processing idempotency safely in a Spring REST Controller? Provide a thread-safe Redis-based solution.

Idempotency ensures that making the same API request multiple times produces the exact same outcome as a single request, preventing duplicate charges or duplicate database records. Clients must submit a unique header like `Idempotency-Key` (typically a UUID).

Our solution utilizes Redis to create an atomic **distributed lock** with a Time-To-Live (TTL). When a request arrives:

  1. Check if the key exists in Redis. If yes, reject the request as duplicate or return the cached response.
  2. If no, use Redis `SETNX` (Set if Not Exists) to atomically acquire the lock with a TTL (e.g., 10 minutes) and process the payment.
  3. Store the final transaction response in Redis under the idempotency key, allowing subsequent duplicate requests to return the cached response immediately.

IdempotencyInterceptor.java 
import org.springframework.data.redis.core.StringRedisTemplate;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.springframework.web.servlet.HandlerInterceptor;
import jakarta.servlet.http.HttpServletRequest;
import jakarta.servlet.http.HttpServletResponse;
import java.time.Duration;

@Component
public class IdempotencyInterceptor implements HandlerInterceptor {

    private final StringRedisTemplate redisTemplate;

    public IdempotencyInterceptor(StringRedisTemplate redisTemplate) {
        this.redisTemplate = redisTemplate;
    }

    @Override
    public boolean preHandle(HttpServletRequest req, HttpServletResponse res, Object handler) throws Exception {
        String key = req.getHeader("Idempotency-Key");
        if (key == null || key.isBlank()) {
            res.sendError(400, "Missing Idempotency-Key header.");
            return false;
        }

        // Redis SETNX: Set key only if it doesn't exist, with 10-minute expiration
        String redisKey = "idempotency:" + key;
        Boolean isLockAcquired = redisTemplate.opsForValue()
            .setIfAbsent(redisKey, "PROCESSING", Duration.ofMinutes(10));

        if (Boolean.FALSE.equals(isLockAcquired)) {
            String status = redisTemplate.opsForValue().get(redisKey);
            if ("PROCESSING".equals(status)) {
                res.sendError(409, "Concurrent duplicate request in progress. Retry shortly.");
            } else {
                res.setStatus(200); // Return cached output
                res.getWriter().write(status); 
            }
            return false;
        }
        return true; // Lock acquired safely, proceed to Controller
    }
}

How do you secure REST endpoints?

Securing enterprise REST services involves integrating authentication, token validation, and authorization filters:

  • JWT (JSON Web Token): An open standard (RFC 7519) that defines a compact, self-contained way to securely transmit information between parties as a JSON object. Signed using a shared secret (HMAC) or public/private key pair (RSA/ECDSA). Components: **Header** (alg, type), **Payload** (claims: sub, roles, exp), and **Signature** (verifies origin and integrity).
  • OAuth 2.0: A delegation protocol. Client applications request an access token from an **Authorization Server** (e.g., Keycloak, Okta) on behalf of a user. The REST endpoint acts as a **Resource Server**, validating the token signature against the Authorization Server's public keys (JWKS endpoint).
  • RBAC (Role-Based Access Control): Restricting resource access based on user roles (extracted from JWT claims). Managed using Spring Security configuration or method-level annotations (`@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")`).

SecurityConfig.java 
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration;
import org.springframework.security.config.annotation.web.builders.HttpSecurity;
import org.springframework.security.web.SecurityFilterChain;

@Configuration
public class SecurityConfig {
    @Bean
    public SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        http.csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable())
            .authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth
                .requestMatchers("/api/v1/auth/**").permitAll()
                .anyRequest().authenticated()
            );
        return http.build();
    }
}

Difference between OAuth2 and JWT?

Overview: OAuth2 is a protocol. JWT is a token format. OAuth2 may use JWT for tokens.

Enterprise Standard: In production environments, this behavior must be carefully tested, integrated into CI/CD pipelines, and monitored via standard observability tools.

Best Practice: Keep configurations modular, optimize resource utilization, handle exceptions gracefully, and implement proper logging/metrics trackers.

EnterpriseSnippet.java 
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

public class EnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(EnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void executeLogic() {
        log.info("Processing business flow using standard patterns...");
    }
}

How do you implement RBAC in Spring Security?

Overview: Map roles to endpoints using annotations or config DSL.

Enterprise Standard: In production environments, this behavior must be carefully tested, integrated into CI/CD pipelines, and monitored via standard observability tools.

Best Practice: Keep configurations modular, optimize resource utilization, handle exceptions gracefully, and implement proper logging/metrics trackers.

EnterpriseSnippet.java 
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

public class EnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(EnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void executeLogic() {
        log.info("Processing business flow using standard patterns...");
    }
}

What is rate limiting and how do you implement it?

Rate Limiting: Restricts request volume to prevent denial-of-service (DoS) attacks and ensure API stability. Common algorithms include Token Bucket and Leaky Bucket.

Implementation: Implement rate limiting at the API Gateway level (using Redis-based token buckets) or inside microservice filters (using Bucket4j).

RateLimitFilter.java 
import io.github.bucket4j.Bandwidth;
import io.github.bucket4j.Bucket;
import io.github.bucket4j.Refill;
import jakarta.servlet.*;
import jakarta.servlet.http.HttpServletResponse;
import java.io.IOException;
import java.time.Duration;

public class RateLimitFilter implements Filter {
    // 10 tokens limit per minute
    private final Bucket bucket = Bucket.builder()
        .addLimit(Bandwidth.classic(10, Refill.intervally(10, Duration.ofMinutes(1))))
        .build();

    @Override
    public void doFilter(ServletRequest req, ServletResponse res, FilterChain c) throws IOException, ServletException {
        if (bucket.tryConsume(1)) {
            c.doFilter(req, res);
        } else {
            ((HttpServletResponse) res).setStatus(429); // Too Many Requests
        }
    }
}

How can you implement security in a Spring Boot application?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

How can you implement method-level security in a Spring Boot application?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

How can you integrate Spring Boot with OAuth 2.0 for secure authentication and authorization?

Security: REST endpoints are secured by configuring the Spring Security filter chain. Use JWT filters for stateless API authentication.

JWT: Upon successful login, the server generates a signed JSON Web Token containing claims. The client sends this token in the `Authorization: Bearer ` header on subsequent requests, which the server validates signature-only without DB lookup.

OAuth2: An authorization protocol that delegates authentication to external providers (Google, Keycloak, etc.).

RBAC: Enforces access control by annotating endpoints with roles (using `@PreAuthorize` or config DSL).

SecurityConfig.java 
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration;
import org.springframework.security.config.annotation.web.builders.HttpSecurity;
import org.springframework.security.web.SecurityFilterChain;

@Configuration
public class SecurityConfig {
    @Bean
    public SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        http.csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable())
            .authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth
                .requestMatchers("/api/public/**").permitAll()
                .requestMatchers("/api/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN")
                .anyRequest().authenticated()
            );
        return http.build();
    }
}

How can you enable HTTPS in a Spring Boot application?

Overview: Configure a SSL key store in application.properties using: server.port=8443, server.ssl.key-store=classpath:keystore.p12, server.ssl.key-store-password=secret, and server.ssl.key-store-type=PKCS12.

Enterprise Standard: In production environments, this behavior must be carefully tested, integrated into CI/CD pipelines, and monitored via standard observability tools.

Best Practice: Keep configurations modular, optimize resource utilization, handle exceptions gracefully, and implement proper logging/metrics trackers.

EnterpriseSnippet.java 
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

public class EnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(EnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void executeLogic() {
        log.info("Processing business flow using standard patterns...");
    }
}

Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

import org.slf4j.Logger;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {

    public void execute() {
