How to Enable Transactions in Spring and What Are Their Benefits?

Spring Transaction Management: Handles database commits and rollbacks declaratively using AOP proxies. When a method annotated with `@Transactional` is invoked, Spring intercepts the call, obtains a connection, and manages the transaction lifecycle.

REQUIRED (Default): Joins the existing transaction if present, or creates a new one if none exists.

REQUIRES_NEW: Suspends the active transaction and starts a completely independent transaction, which commits or rolls back independently of the parent transaction.

NESTED: Starts a nested transaction using database savepoints. If the nested transaction fails, it rolls back to the savepoint without affecting the outer transaction's progress.

TransactionPropagationDemo.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;
import org.springframework.transaction.annotation.Propagation;
import org.springframework.transaction.annotation.Transactional;

@Service
public class TransactionPropagationDemo {

    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRED)
    public void mainWorkflow() {
        // Parent transaction context
        logActivity(); // If logActivity fails, mainWorkflow can still commit if caught
    }

    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
    public void logActivity() {
        // Executes in a separate, isolated database transaction
    }
}

What Are Aspect, Advice, Pointcut, and JoinPoint in AOP?

SQL Joins: Combine rows from multiple tables using matching column keys.

INNER JOIN: Returns rows only when there is a match in both tables. If a row in the left table has no matching row in the right table, it is excluded.

LEFT JOIN: Returns all rows from the left table, plus matched rows from the right table. Unmatched right-side columns are filled with NULL values.

JoinsDemo.sql 
-- INNER JOIN: returns companies with employees
SELECT c.name, e.name 
FROM Company c
INNER JOIN Employee e ON c.id = e.company_id;

-- LEFT JOIN: returns all companies, even those without employees
SELECT c.name, e.name 
FROM Company c
LEFT JOIN Employee e ON c.id = e.company_id;
Logic & Optimization

Algorithms & Data Structures

ArrayList vs LinkedList (reversing a linked list in-place), HashMap internals (bucket indexing, treeification), binary search, QuickSort, BFS vs DFS, and high-performance TTL LRU cache.

Implement in-place LinkedList reversal, Binary Search, and explain BFS vs DFS graph traversals.

Key algorithmic patterns frequently used in software development:

  • In-Place LinkedList Reversal: Reverse a singly linked list iteratively by maintaining three pointers: `prev`, `curr`, and `next`. Point `curr.next` to `prev`, then move the pointers forward. O(N) time complexity, O(1) auxiliary space complexity.
  • Binary Search: A divide-and-conquer search algorithm on a **sorted array**. Compare the search key with the array's midpoint. If they match, return the index. If the key is smaller, narrow search to the left half; if larger, to the right half. O(log N) search time complexity.
  • BFS vs DFS Graph Traversals:
    • **BFS (Breadth-First Search)**: Explores a graph level-by-level, visiting all neighbor nodes first. Implemented using a **Queue**. Best for finding the shortest path in unweighted graphs.
    • **DFS (Depth-First Search)**: Explores a branch as deep as possible before backtracking. Implemented using recursion or a **Stack**. Best for topological sorting, finding connected components, and resolving dependencies.

LinkedListInPlaceReversal.java 
public class LinkedListInPlaceReversal {
    static class Node {
        int val;
        Node next;
        Node(int val) { this.val = val; }
    }

    public static Node reverse(Node head) {
        Node prev = null;
        Node curr = head;
        Node next = null;

        while (curr != null) {
            next = curr.next; // Store next node
            curr.next = prev; // Reverse current node link
            prev = curr;      // Move prev pointer forward
            curr = next;      // Move curr pointer forward
        }
        return prev; // New head of reversed list
    }
}

Implement a thread-safe, high-performance Least Recently Used (LRU) cache with a Time-To-Live (TTL) expiration mechanism in pure Java.

A classic LRU cache maintains O(1) reads and writes by combining a **HashMap** (for lookup) with a **Doubly Linked List** (to track usage order). When a key is accessed, we move its node to the head of the list. When the cache is full, we evict the node at the tail of the list.

To support a Time-To-Live (TTL) expiration mechanism, we store the creation/write timestamp in each cache node. During read access, we check if the current time exceeds the node's TTL. If expired, we remove the node immediately and return null. We ensure thread safety by wrapping accesses inside a ReentrantReadWriteLock, permitting multiple concurrent readers but isolating writers.

ConcurrentLruCache.java 
import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.Map;
import java.util.concurrent.locks.ReentrantReadWriteLock;

public class ConcurrentLruCache<K, V> {
    private static class CacheNode<K, V> {
        K key;
        V value;
        long expiryTime;
        CacheNode<K, V> prev, next;
        
        CacheNode(K key, V value, long ttlMs) {
            this.key = key;
            this.value = value;
            this.expiryTime = System.currentTimeMillis() + ttlMs;
        }
    }

    private final int capacity;
    private final Map<K, CacheNode<K, V>> map = new HashMap<>();
    private final CacheNode<K, V> head, tail;
    private final ReentrantReadWriteLock lock = new ReentrantReadWriteLock();

    public ConcurrentLruCache(int capacity) {
        this.capacity = capacity;
        this.head = new CacheNode<>(null, null, 0);
        this.tail = new CacheNode<>(null, null, 0);
        head.next = tail;
        tail.prev = head;
    }

    public V get(K key) {
        lock.readLock().lock();
        try {
            CacheNode<K, V> node = map.get(key);
            if (node == null) return null;
            
            // Check TTL expiration
            if (System.currentTimeMillis() > node.expiryTime) {
                // Upgrade to write lock to evict expired item
                lock.readLock().unlock();
                lock.writeLock().lock();
                try {
                    evictNode(node);
                } finally {
                    lock.writeLock().unlock();
                    lock.readLock().lock(); // Downgrade lock
                }
                return null;
            }
            
            // Move accessed node to head (indicates recently used)
            lock.readLock().unlock();
            lock.writeLock().lock();
            try {
                moveToHead(node);
            } finally {
                lock.writeLock().unlock();
                lock.readLock().lock();
            }
            return node.value;
        } finally {
            lock.readLock().unlock();
        }
    }

    public void put(K key, V value, long ttlMs) {
        lock.writeLock().lock();
        try {
            CacheNode<K, V> existing = map.get(key);
            if (existing != null) {
                existing.value = value;
                existing.expiryTime = System.currentTimeMillis() + ttlMs;
                moveToHead(existing);
                return;
            }

            if (map.size() >= capacity) {
                // Evict least recently used (tail.prev)
                evictNode(tail.prev);
            }

            CacheNode<K, V> newNode = new CacheNode<>(key, value, ttlMs);
            map.put(key, newNode);
            addNodeAtHead(newNode);
        } finally {
            lock.writeLock().unlock();
        }
    }

    private void addNodeAtHead(CacheNode<K, V> node) {
        node.next = head.next;
        node.prev = head;
        head.next.prev = node;
        head.next = node;
    }

    private void removeNode(CacheNode<K, V> node) {
        node.prev.next = node.next;
        node.next.prev = node.prev;
    }

    private void moveToHead(CacheNode<K, V> node) {
        removeNode(node);
        addNodeAtHead(node);
    }

    private void evictNode(CacheNode<K, V> node) {
        removeNode(node);
        map.remove(node.key);
    }
}

Explain HashMap internals, bucket indexing, collision resolution, and Java 8 treeification.

HashMap is built on an array of nodes (buckets): `Node[] table`.

Core Operations:
  1. Bucket Indexing: When you call `put(key, value)`, HashMap calculates the key's hash using `hash = key.hashCode() ^ (hash >>> 16)` (spreads high bits down to reduce collisions). The bucket index is calculated using bitwise AND: `index = hash & (n - 1)` (where `n` is the array length, which is always a power of 2).
  2. Collision Resolution: If two keys map to the same index, they are stored in a linked list at that bucket node (chaining).
  3. Java 8 Treeification: If a linked list at a single bucket grows beyond a threshold (TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 8) and the total table capacity is at least 64, HashMap converts the linked list into a balanced **Red-Black Tree**. This improves search time from O(N) to O(log N) for collided buckets. If the size shrinks below 6, it untreeifies back to a list.

Can patterns introduce complexity? How do you balance it?

Overview: Yes. Apply only where there's recurring benefit. Avoid over-engineering.

Enterprise Standard: In production environments, this behavior must be carefully tested, integrated into CI/CD pipelines, and monitored via standard observability tools.

Best Practice: Keep configurations modular, optimize resource utilization, handle exceptions gracefully, and implement proper logging/metrics trackers.

EnterpriseSnippet.java 
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

public class EnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(EnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void executeLogic() {
        log.info("Processing business flow using standard patterns...");
    }
}

Write a function to check if a string is a palindrome.

Two-Pointer Scan: To check if a string is a palindrome, initialize one pointer at the start and another at the end of the string. Increment the start pointer and decrement the end pointer, comparing character values at each step.

Time Complexity: Requires at most N/2 comparisons, executing in O(N) time.

Space Complexity: Requires only two pointer variables, executing in O(1) space.

PalindromeChecker.java 
public class PalindromeChecker {
    public boolean isPalindrome(String str) {
        if (str == null) return false;
        int left = 0;
        int right = str.length() - 1;
        while (left < right) {
            if (str.charAt(left) != str.charAt(right)) {
                return false;
            }
            left++;
            right--;
        }
        return true;
    }
}

Explain binary search algorithm.

Divide and Conquer: Binary search finds an element in a **sorted array** by repeatedly dividing the search space in half. Compare the target value to the middle element; if they match, return the index. If target is smaller, narrow search space to the left half; otherwise, narrow to the right half.

Time Complexity: Halving the array search space at each iteration yields O(log N) complexity.

Space Complexity: Iterative implementations use O(1) auxiliary space.

BinarySearch.java 
public class BinarySearch {
    public int search(int[] arr, int target) {
        int low = 0;
        int high = arr.length - 1;
        while (low <= high) {
            int mid = low + (high - low) / 2; // Prevents integer overflow
            if (arr[mid] == target) return mid;
            if (arr[mid] < target) low = mid + 1;
            else high = mid - 1;
        }
        return -1; // Target not found
    }
}

How does QuickSort work?

Partitioning Strategy: QuickSort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm. It selects a 'pivot' element, partitions the array so elements smaller than pivot precede it while larger elements follow it, and recurses on both partitions.

Time Complexity: Average execution takes O(N log N) time. Worst-case takes O(N^2) if the pivot partition is extremely unbalanced.

Space Complexity: Requires O(log N) stack space for recursion frames.

QuickSort.java 
public class QuickSort {
    public void sort(int[] arr, int low, int high) {
        if (low < high) {
            int pivotIndex = partition(arr, low, high);
            sort(arr, low, pivotIndex - 1);  // Recurse left
            sort(arr, pivotIndex + 1, high); // Recurse right
        }
    }

    private int partition(int[] arr, int low, int high) {
        int pivot = arr[high];
        int i = low - 1;
        for (int j = low; j < high; j++) {
            if (arr[j] < pivot) {
                i++;
                swap(arr, i, j);
            }
        }
        swap(arr, i + 1, high);
        return i + 1;
    }

    private void swap(int[] arr, int i, int j) {
        int temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp;
    }
}

When is a heap used in Java?

Binary Heap: A complete binary tree that maintains the heap property. In a Max Heap, parent nodes are greater than or equal to their children; in a Min Heap, parent nodes are less than or equal to their children.

Java PriorityQueue: Under the hood, Java's `PriorityQueue` implements a balanced Min-Heap, sorting elements according to their natural order or via a custom Comparator.

Operations: Insertion and removal of the root element (poll) both execute in O(log N) time, while peek runs in O(1) time.

MinHeapDemo.java 
import java.util.PriorityQueue;

public class MinHeapDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // PriorityQueue acts as a Min-Heap by default
        PriorityQueue<Integer> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>();
        minHeap.offer(15);
        minHeap.offer(5);
        minHeap.offer(10);

        System.out.println("Minimum element: " + minHeap.peek()); // 5
        System.out.println("Removed: " + minHeap.poll()); // 5, heap re-balances
        System.out.println("Next Minimum: " + minHeap.peek()); // 10
    }
}

Implement LRU cache.

LRU (Least Recently Used) Cache: A cache eviction strategy that removes the least recently accessed items first when capacity limits are exceeded.

LinkedHashMap Implementation: Java's LinkedHashMap can be configured to maintain access-order (instead of insertion-order) and override removeEldestEntry() to handle eviction automatically.

Time Complexity: Providing both O(1) lookups and O(1) updates is achieved by combining a Hash Map with a Doubly-Linked List.

LruCache.java 
import java.util.LinkedHashMap;
import java.util.Map;

public class LruCache<K, V> extends LinkedHashMap<K, V> {
    private final int capacity;

    public LruCache(int capacity) {
        // True enables access-order tracking
        super(capacity, 0.75f, true);
        this.capacity = capacity;
    }

    @Override
    protected boolean removeEldestEntry(Map.Entry<K, V> eldest) {
        return size() > capacity; // Automatically evict eldest on overflow
    }
}

What’s a Trie? Use case?

Prefix Tree: A Trie is a specialized search tree structure designed for rapid prefix match operations. Each node represents a single character, and words share common prefix paths.

Use Cases: Ideal for autocomplete engines, spelling checkers, IP routing lookups, and T9 predictive text search configurations.

Performance: Search operations execute in O(L) time, where L is the length of the target string, bypassing search complexity scaling based on dictionary size.

TrieDemo.java 
import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.Map;

class TrieNode {
    Map<Character, TrieNode> children = new HashMap<>();
    boolean isEndOfWord = false;
}

public class TrieDemo {
    private final TrieNode root = new TrieNode();

    public void insert(String word) {
        TrieNode curr = root;
        for (char ch : word.toCharArray()) {
            curr = curr.children.computeIfAbsent(ch, c -> new TrieNode());
        }
        curr.isEndOfWord = true;
    }
}

How do you detect a cycle in a graph?

Cycle Detection: In directed graphs, a cycle exists if there is a back-edge during traversal. This is resolved using a Depth-First Search (DFS) tracking vertex visiting states.

Three-State Coloring: Mark nodes with states: (1) Unvisited: not yet processed; (2) Visiting: currently in the active DFS recursion stack; (3) Visited: fully processed.

Cycle Identification: If during DFS traversal of a node's neighbors we encounter a neighbor marked as **Visiting**, a back-edge is present, indicating a cycle.

CycleDetector.java 
import java.util.*;

public class CycleDetector {
    public boolean hasCycle(int v, List<List<Integer>> adj) {
        int[] visited = new int[v]; // 0: unvisited, 1: visiting, 2: visited
        for (int i = 0; i < v; i++) {
            if (visited[i] == 0) {
                if (dfs(i, adj, visited)) return true;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }

    private boolean dfs(int node, List<List<Integer>> adj, int[] visited) {
        visited[node] = 1; // Mark as visiting
        for (int neighbor : adj.get(node)) {
            if (visited[neighbor] == 1) return true; // Cycle detected
            if (visited[neighbor] == 0) {
                if (dfs(neighbor, adj, visited)) return true;
            }
        }
        visited[node] = 2; // Mark as fully visited
        return false;
    }
}

How do you find the intersection of two arrays?

Array Intersection: Finding the common elements between two arrays can be optimized using a HashSet to store elements of the first array.

Hash Lookup Efficiency: Iterate through the second array, checking if elements exist in the hash set. If found, add them to the result and remove them from the set to prevent duplicates.

Time Complexity: Building and querying the Hash Set results in O(N + M) complexity, which is far faster than nested O(N * M) loop structures.

ArrayIntersection.java 
import java.util.HashSet;
import java.util.Set;

public class ArrayIntersection {
    public int[] intersect(int[] nums1, int[] nums2) {
        Set<Integer> set = new HashSet<>();
        for (int num : nums1) set.add(num);

        Set<Integer> intersect = new HashSet<>();
        for (int num : nums2) {
            if (set.contains(num)) {
                intersect.add(num);
            }
        }

        return intersect.stream().mapToInt(Integer::intValue).toArray();
    }
}

What is dynamic programming?

Dynamic Programming (DP): An algorithmic technique that solves complex problems by breaking them down into simpler, overlapping subproblems, storing solved states to avoid redundant calculations.

Memoization (Top-Down): Extends standard recursive functions by checking a lookup cache (memo table) before executing calculations, returning results instantly if already solved.

Tabulation (Bottom-Up): An iterative approach that solves subproblems first, building up solutions sequentially from the bottom to populate a DP table.

FibonacciDp.java 
public class FibonacciDp {
    // 1. Top-Down Memoization
    public int fibMemo(int n, int[] memo) {
        if (n <= 1) return n;
        if (memo[n] != 0) return memo[n];
        memo[n] = fibMemo(n - 1, memo) + fibMemo(n - 2, memo);
        return memo[n];
    }

    // 2. Bottom-Up Tabulation
    public int fibTab(int n) {
        if (n <= 1) return n;
        int[] dp = new int[n + 1];
        dp[0] = 0; dp[1] = 1;
        for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
            dp[i] = dp[i - 1] + dp[i - 2];
        }
        return dp[n];
    }
}

What is memoization?

Overview: Cache results of expensive function calls.

Enterprise Standard: In production environments, this behavior must be carefully tested, integrated into CI/CD pipelines, and monitored via standard observability tools.

Best Practice: Keep configurations modular, optimize resource utilization, handle exceptions gracefully, and implement proper logging/metrics trackers.

EnterpriseSnippet.java 
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

public class EnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(EnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void executeLogic() {
        log.info("Processing business flow using standard patterns...");
    }
}
Frontend Architecture

React Framework (3 YOE)

React rendering lifecycle, virtual DOM, React hooks (useState, useEffect, useMemo, useCallback), routing, and state management (Zustand stores, Context API, Redux comparisons).

What are React Hooks and Custom Hooks? Explain the Rules of Hooks and how hooks encapsulate stateful logic.

React Hooks: Functions that let functional components tap into React's state and lifecycle features. They do not store state directly; React uses an internal execution order list to match each hook with its state slot on the component's Fiber node.

Rules of Hooks: (1) Only call hooks at the top level: Never call hooks inside loops, conditions, or nested functions to ensure the hooks linked list order remains identical on every render. (2) Only call hooks from React functions: Call them from functional components or custom hooks, never from vanilla Javascript helper functions.

Custom Hooks: Custom hooks are reusable JavaScript functions prefixed with "use" that call other hooks. They extract and encapsulate stateful logic (e.g. form validation, data fetching) so it can be shared across multiple components.

State Isolation: Custom hooks do not share state data; each call to a custom hook generates completely isolated state instances within the host component's Fiber node context.

useCustomFetch.js 
import { useState, useEffect } from 'react';

// Custom Hook encapsulating state, loading, and error boundaries
export function useFetch(url) {
    const [data, setData] = useState(null);
    const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true);
    const [error, setError] = useState(null);

    useEffect(() => {
        let active = true;
        setLoading(true);

        fetch(url)
            .then(res => {
                if (!res.ok) throw new Error("Network request failed");
                return res.json();
            })
            .then(json => {
                if (active) setData(json);
            })
            .catch(err => {
                if (active) setError(err.message);
            })
            .finally(() => {
                if (active) setLoading(false);
            });

        return () => {
            active = false;
        };
    }, [url]);

    // Return encapsulated state
    return { data, loading, error };
}

Compare the Virtual DOM vs Real DOM. What is the difference between a React Node and a DOM Node? How do you perform parentNode traversal using Refs?

Virtual DOM vs Real DOM: The Real DOM is the browser's hierarchical representation of the document, where updates are slow because they trigger expensive page layout calculations. The Virtual DOM is a lightweight, in-memory copy. React compares Virtual DOM changes using a diffing algorithm and updates the Real DOM in a single transaction.

React Node vs DOM Node: A DOM Node is a physical object inside the browser document tree. A React Node is a lightweight representation of a React element, string, fragment, or portal, which gets reconciled into a DOM Node at runtime.

Refs & parentNode Traversal: Direct DOM manipulation is discouraged in React as it bypasses the virtual DOM. However, when traversal is required (e.g. positioning tooltips, measuring parent layout), use a useRef to get a direct reference to the child element, and access ref.current.parentNode.

useLayoutEffect: If your parentNode traversal requires measuring DOM layout properties (like parent height/width) to perform synchronous visual updates, execute it inside useLayoutEffect instead of useEffect to prevent visual flickering.

DomNodeRefTraversal.jsx 
import React, { useRef, useLayoutEffect, useState } from 'react';

export default function DomNodeRefTraversal() {
    const childRef = useRef(null);
    const [parentBackground, setParentBackground] = useState('');

    useLayoutEffect(() => {
        // Accessing the real browser DOM Node from the ref
        if (childRef.current) {
            // Traversal up the DOM tree using parentNode
            const parentElement = childRef.current.parentNode;
            
            if (parentElement) {
                // Read parent styles directly
                const computedStyle = window.getComputedStyle(parentElement);
                setParentBackground(computedStyle.backgroundColor);
            }
        }
    }, []); // Runs synchronously after DOM updates, before paint

    return (
        <div style={{ padding: '20px', backgroundColor: '#e0f2fe' }}>
            <h4>Parent Container</h4>
            <div ref={childRef} style={{ padding: '10px', color: '#1e3a8a' }}>
                Child Element - Parent Background detected as: {parentBackground}
            </div>
        </div>
    );
}

How do useState and useEffect work under the hood? Explain state batching, functional updates, and how to handle async/await operations inside useEffect safely.

useState Internals: React preserves component state across renders by storing hooks on a linked list attached to the component's Fiber node. Multiple state updates triggered within the same task (e.g. click handler) are batched together into a single render to optimize performance. To ensure updates are made against the most current state, always use functional state updates: setCount(prev => prev + 1).

useEffect Lifecycle: Runs asynchronously after the render has been committed and the browser has painted the screen. If no dependency array is supplied, it executes on every render. If an empty array is provided, it executes once on mount. Cleanups are triggered before the next execution or on unmount.

Async Operations inside useEffect: The effect callback itself cannot be declared as async because it must return either nothing or a cleanup function (async functions return a Promise). Instead, declare a nested async function inside the effect and call it.

Race Conditions & Cleanups: When fetching async data inside effects, network responses can arrive out of order, or after a component has unmounted, leading to memory leaks and state corruption. Resolve this by maintaining an active flag or using an AbortController to discard outstanding operations inside the cleanup function.

AsyncStateEffectDemo.jsx 
import React, { useState, useEffect } from 'react';

export default function AsyncStateEffectDemo({ userId }) {
    const [userData, setUserData] = useState(null);
    const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true);

    useEffect(() => {
        let isCurrent = true; // Flag to prevent race conditions
        const abortController = new AbortController();

        // 1. Declare async function inside the effect
        async function fetchUserProfile() {
            setLoading(true);
            try {
                const response = await fetch(`https://api.valore.com/users/${userId}`, {
                    signal: abortController.signal
                });
                const data = await response.json();
                
                // 2. Only update state if the component is still mounted with this userId
                if (isCurrent) {
                    setUserData(data);
                }
            } catch (error) {
                if (error.name !== 'AbortError') {
                    console.error("Fetch failed", error);
                }
            } finally {
                if (isCurrent) setLoading(false);
            }
        }

        fetchUserProfile();

        // 3. Cleanup function resets flag and aborts active network request
        return () => {
            isCurrent = false;
            abortController.abort();
        };
    }, [userId]); // Executed whenever userId prop changes

    if (loading) return <div>Loading Profile...</div>;
    return <div>User: {userData?.name}</div>;
}

How do you optimize rendering performance in React? Implement an optimized state consumer component with Zustand.

React components re-render whenever their parent re-renders or their state changes. In large applications, unnecessary rendering cycles cause UI lag.

Rendering Optimization Hooks:
  • useMemo: Memoizes expensive computations, executing them only when dependencies change.
  • useCallback: Memoizes function instances, preventing child components from re-rendering due to inline function reference changes.
  • React.memo: A higher-order component that skips rendering a component if its incoming props are unchanged.
State Management: Zustand vs Context API

The Context API causes **all consuming components to re-render** whenever the context value changes, even if a component only consumes a subset of the value. Zustand is a lightweight state management library that solves this. It uses a publish-subscribe model with **selector-based state subscription**, ensuring components re-render only when the specific sliced attribute they select changes.

ZustandDashboardStore.js 
import { create } from 'zustand';

// 1. Create a global Zustand store
export const useDashboardStore = create((set) => ({
    userCount: 150,
    serverStatus: "HEALTHY",
    notifications: [],
    
    incrementUserCount: () => set((state) => ({ userCount: state.userCount + 1 })),
    setServerStatus: (status) => set({ serverStatus: status }),
    addNotification: (msg) => set((state) => ({ 
        notifications: [...state.notifications, { id: Date.now(), msg }] 
    }))
}));

// 2. React Consumer Component with Optimized Selector Subscription
export function UserCounterComponent() {
    // Selector subscription: Component ONLY re-renders when 'userCount' changes!
    const userCount = useDashboardStore((state) => state.userCount);
    const increment = useDashboardStore((state) => state.incrementUserCount);

    console.log("UserCounterComponent rendered!"); // Confirms render isolation

    return (
        <div className="card p-3 border-0 bg-dark text-white rounded">
            <h5>Total Active Users: {userCount}</h5>
            <button className="btn btn-primary btn-sm mt-2" onClick={increment}>
                Add User
            </button>
        </div>
    );
}

Explain the React component lifecycle, virtual DOM reconciliation, and the usage rules of Hooks.

React is built on a declarative programming model with efficient UI updates:

  • Virtual DOM Reconciliation (Fiber): React maintains a lightweight virtual DOM tree in memory. When state changes, React generates a new Virtual DOM tree. The **reconciliation algorithm (Fiber)** compares the new tree with the old tree (diffing) and batches the minimal set of updates required to modify the real DOM, avoiding expensive layout reflows.
  • Rules of Hooks: Hooks (introduced in React 16.8) allow functional components to manage state and lifecycle side effects.
    1. **Only Call Hooks at the Top Level**: Do not call hooks inside loops, conditions, or nested functions. This ensures React executes hooks in the exact same order on every render.
    2. **Only Call Hooks from React Functions**: Call hooks only from React functional components or custom hooks.
  • Core Hooks:
    • `useState`: Manages local component state.
    • `useEffect`: Manages side effects (API calls, subscriptions, DOM mutations). Fires asynchronously after rendering completes.
    • `useRef`: Retains mutable values that do not trigger re-renders, and offers direct access to DOM nodes.

ComponentLifecycle.jsx 
import React, { useState, useEffect } from 'react';

export default function ComponentLifecycle() {
    const [status, setStatus] = useState('Idle');

    useEffect(() => {
        setStatus('Mounted');
        console.log('Component mounted (reconciliation complete)');
        
        return () => {
            console.log('Component unmounted (cleanup hook executed)');
        };
    }, []); // Empty dependency array run-once constraint

    return <div>Component Status: {status}</div>;
}

Explain the concept of reactive programming in Spring Boot with Spring WebFlux.

Reactive Architecture: Spring WebFlux is a non-blocking reactive web framework running on Netty, bypassing the thread-per-request model.

Mono & Flux: Mono represents a reactive publisher that emits 0 or 1 item. Flux represents a publisher that emits a stream of 0 to N items.

Event Loop: Netty utilizes a small number of event loop threads to handle thousands of concurrent requests non-blockingly. Blocking operations (like legacy JDBC) must be offloaded to separate schedulers to prevent event loop starvation.

ReactiveController.java 
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.GetMapping;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RestController;
import reactor.core.publisher.Flux;
import java.time.Duration;

@RestController
public class ReactiveController {
    
    @GetMapping(value = "/api/stream", produces = "text/event-stream")
    public Flux<String> streamData() {
        return Flux.interval(Duration.ofSeconds(1))
                   .map(sec -> "Pulse: " + sec)
                   .take(10); // Safe non-blocking stream
    }
}

Explain the concept of reactive data access in Spring Boot using Spring Data R2DBC.

R2DBC: Reactive Relational Database Connectivity. It brings reactive, non-blocking database drivers to SQL engines (like PostgreSQL, MySQL), replacing blocking JDBC drivers.

Concurrency Improvement: Because R2DBC uses reactive pipelines, database worker threads do not block, allowing a small number of application threads to handle massive database request volumes.

R2dbcConfig.java 
import io.r2dbc.spi.ConnectionFactory;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration;
import org.springframework.data.r2dbc.config.AbstractR2dbcConfiguration;
import org.springframework.data.r2dbc.repository.config.EnableR2dbcRepositories;

@Configuration
@EnableR2dbcRepositories
public class R2dbcConfig extends AbstractR2dbcConfiguration {
    @Override
    public ConnectionFactory connectionFactory() {
        // Auto-configured based on connection properties
        return null; 
    }
}

Explain the concept of reactive messaging with Spring Boot and Apache Kafka Streams.

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

What Is Reactive Programming?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}

What Are the Disadvantages of Using Reactive Streams?

Enterprise Development Standards: This question covers core Java Full-Stack concepts including configuration, dependency injection, and framework architectures. Standard Spring Boot practices require proper layering (Controller, Service, Repository), exception translation, and configuration isolation.

Component Architecture: Ensure components are loosely coupled, unit-testable using Mockito, and follow SOLID design principles. Always externalize configuration parameters into application properties or environment variables.

Operational Best Practices: Bind logging variables dynamically via MDC, route network requests securely via filter chains, and measure JVM health continuously using Actuator health probes.

StandardEnterpriseComponent.java 
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;

@Component
public class StandardEnterpriseComponent {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(StandardEnterpriseComponent.class);

    public void execute() {
        log.info("Executing standard enterprise business logic flow...");
    }
}
